proposals.title: Modeling Dendrimer-Packed Lipid Bilayers as Polymer Brushes: Predicting Membrane Structure and Mechanics
proposals.motivation: Dendrimers and hyperbranched polymers show promise for creating synthetic membranes with tailored permeability and stability, as seen in recent work on polymeric bicontinuous microemulsions. However, predicting the structure-property relationships of membranes where lipid bilayers are densely packed with dendritic molecules remains a challenge. Applying well-established polymer brush theory, developed for tethered polymer chains at interfaces, offers a powerful, quantitative framework to model these complex systems and guide their design.
proposals.hypothesis: The structural and mechanical properties of a lipid bilayer densely functionalized with dendrimers can be quantitatively described by adapting self-consistent field theory (SCFT) for polymer brushes, where the dendrimer generation and grafting density dictate the brush height, lateral pressure profile, and membrane bending modulus.
proposals.method: We will adapt numerical self-consistent field theory (SCFT) algorithms, specifically designed for polymer brushes and ternary blends, to model a dendrimer-grafted bilayer. The model will treat lipid tails as a grafted layer and dendrimers as grafted, branched polymers of variable generation (G1-G4). We will compute the equilibrium brush height, density profiles, and lateral pressure distribution as a function of dendrimer generation and grafting density, comparing predictions to coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations of model systems. Key parameters will be informed by the group's expertise in poloxamer and triblock polymer phase behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first theoretical framework connecting dendrimer architecture to synthetic membrane mechanics, enabling the rational design of non-lamellar, dendrimer-stabilized phases. It will extend polymer brush theory to a new class of branched, membrane-active materials, offering predictive power for tuning properties like stiffness and permeability in biomedical and separation applications.
proposals.novelty: While polymer brush theory is mature for linear chains, its application to model dendrimer-packed bilayers as a brush system is novel. The approach uniquely combines SCFT for branched architectures with membrane biophysics to predict emergent mechanical properties from molecular architecture.

proposals.title: Universal Critical Molecular Weight for Network Formation in Block Copolymer Hydrogels and Thermoplastic Elastomers
proposals.motivation: The critical molecular weight (Mc) for network formation is a foundational concept in polymer physics, dictating the onset of entanglements and mechanical integrity. For linear homopolymers, Mc is well-established, but its universality in complex, self-assembling block copolymer systems—such as those forming physical gels or thermoplastic elastomers—remains untested. Understanding if Mc is governed purely by chain entanglement or is modulated by architecture (e.g., ABA vs. BAB, multiblock) and chemistry (e.g., PPO vs. PEO, hydrophobic block type) is crucial for the rational design of soft materials with tailored mechanical properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the critical molecular weight for network formation in physically associating block copolymers is a universal function of the entanglement molecular weight of the mid-block, independent of overall copolymer architecture and the specific chemistry of the end-blocks.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of well-defined ABA, BAB, and multiblock copolymers using poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) and poly(propylene oxide) (PPO) chemistries, systematically varying the molecular weight of the mid-block (the continuous matrix) while keeping end-block lengths constant. Network formation will be assessed via temperature-dependent rheology (gel point, plateau modulus) and small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) to correlate mechanical percolation with ordered microstructure. The approach builds on established methods for P407 (PEO-PPO-PEO) hydrogels and extends to reverse poloxamers (PPO-PEO-PPO) and photocrosslinkable systems to decouple architectural from chemical effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental design rule for block copolymer networks, clarifying the relative roles of entanglements versus specific interactions in gelation. It will provide predictive guidelines for designing next-generation injectable biomaterials, thermoplastic elastomers, and membranes with precisely controlled mechanical response.
proposals.novelty: While Mc is known for homopolymers, systematically testing its universality across block copolymer architectures and chemistries—especially using model systems from the Bates group's expertise in poloxamers and ternary blends—is a novel and direct test of core polymer physics in complex, self-assembling materials.

proposals.title: Mapping the Complete Aqueous Phase Diagram of Polybutadiene-b-Poly(ethylene oxide) Diblock Copolymers: From Micelles to Bicontinuous Gels
proposals.motivation: The aqueous self-assembly of amphiphilic diblock copolymers like PB-PEO is fundamental to designing soft materials for drug delivery, tissue engineering, and templating. While the phase behavior of PEO-PPO-PEO (Pluronic) systems is well-studied, the more hydrophobic and chemically distinct PB-PEO system lacks a comprehensive morphological map. Establishing its full phase diagram is critical for rationally engineering nanostructures with targeted mechanical properties and transport characteristics, analogous to the foundational work on block copolymer melts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PB-PEO in water will exhibit a rich phase diagram featuring spherical and cylindrical micelles, vesicles, and potentially bicontinuous morphologies (e.g., gyroid) at intermediate PEO fractions and concentrations, with transitions governed by the interplay of core-chain stretching, corona repulsion, and interfacial tension.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PB-PEO diblock copolymers via anionic polymerization, varying PB and PEO block lengths systematically. Aqueous dispersions will be prepared across a full composition matrix (polymer concentration 1-50 wt%, PEO weight fraction 0.1-0.9). Morphology will be characterized using temperature-controlled small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and cryogenic transmission electron microscopy (cryo-TEM). Rheological measurements will identify gel boundaries and mechanical properties. Phase boundaries will be determined by analyzing SAXS patterns with established form and structure factor models for block copolymer micelles and ordered phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete experimental phase diagram for PB-PEO/water, a model system with greater hydrophobic contrast than PPO-based systems. It will establish design rules linking molecular architecture to nanostructure and rheology in aqueous media, enabling the targeted creation of robust gels, membranes, and carriers. The data will also serve as a critical benchmark for theoretical models of amphiphile self-assembly.
proposals.novelty: The systematic mapping of the PB-PEO/water system is novel, as prior work has focused on PPO-based copolymers or ternary blends. The explicit search for and characterization of complex bicontinuous network phases in this simple binary aqueous system, inspired by recent work on polymeric bicontinuous microemulsions, represents a significant advance beyond conventional micellar phase diagrams.

proposals.title: Thermally-Triggered, Muscle-Mimetic Actuators from Hierarchically Structured Triblock Copolymer Blends
proposals.motivation: The development of soft actuators that mimic the hierarchical structure and temperature-responsive performance of biological muscle fibers remains a significant challenge in materials science. Pre-2026 work on poloxamer (PEO-PPO-PEO) hydrogels and ternary polymer blends has demonstrated exquisite control over phase behavior and rheology, but this fundamental understanding has not been translated into robust, solid-state materials with programmable anisotropic mechanical response. Bridging this gap could enable new classes of biomedical and robotic devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by blending a photocrosslinkable, high-χ triblock copolymer with a ductile, low-Tg homopolymer, we can create a hierarchically structured bicontinuous microemulsion that, upon uniaxial alignment, will exhibit a large, reversible, and anisotropic shape-memory effect triggered by a specific thermal transition.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of photocrosslinkable poly(styrene-b-isoprene-b-styrene) (SIS) triblock copolymers with methacrylate end-groups, systematically varying the block lengths to tune the order-disorder transition (ODT). These will be solution-blended with poly(oligo(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate) (POEGMA) of varying side-chain length to form ternary blends. The phase behavior will be mapped using temperature-controlled SAXS and rheology. Selected compositions forming bicontinuous microemulsions will be shear-aligned above the ODT, photocrosslinked to lock the morphology, and solvent-annealed to remove the POEGMA, creating a nanoporous, aligned SIS scaffold. The scaffold will be backfilled with a low-Tg polymer (e.g., poly(butadiene)) to create the final composite fiber.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct materials design pathway from fundamental ternary blend phase diagrams to functional, hierarchically structured actuators. It will provide new insights into how anisotropic, bicontinuous morphologies translate macroscopic strain, and deliver a prototype material system with muscle-like contractile strain and work density, measured via dynamic mechanical analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a thermally responsive, aligned bicontinuous triblock scaffold (derived from ternary blend thermodynamics) with a second, stimulus-responsive polymer phase to create a solid-state composite actuator, moving beyond the well-studied hydrogel systems like P407.

proposals.title: Mapping Non-Equilibrium Pathway Dependence in Triblock Copolymer Hydrogels and Bicontinuous Microemulsions
proposals.motivation: Understanding non-equilibrium dynamics is crucial for designing functional soft materials, yet most studies focus on simple model systems. The Bates group's recent work on poloxamer hydrogels, ternary polymer brushes, and photocrosslinkable bicontinuous microemulsions provides a unique platform to investigate how complex architectures and processing pathways dictate final structure and properties, which is essential for applications like tissue engineering and controlled release.
proposals.hypothesis: The non-equilibrium assembly pathway, dictated by sequence of component addition (e.g., reverse poloxamer addition) or in-situ cross-linking kinetics, will lock in distinct, non-ergodic structural states in complex polymer architectures, leading to tunable and history-dependent mechanical properties that diverge from equilibrium predictions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of well-defined A-B-A triblock copolymers (e.g., PEO-PPO-PEO variants) and photocrosslinkable diblock copolymers for microemulsions. Using temperature-controlled rheology coupled with in-situ small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS), we will systematically vary the assembly pathway, including order of component mixing, thermal quench rates, and UV cross-linking intensity/timing during phase separation. The structural evolution and final state will be correlated with mechanical properties (elastic modulus, yield stress) to construct non-equilibrium state diagrams.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a framework for predicting and controlling the non-equilibrium structure-property relationships in architecturally complex polymer blends and gels. It will provide design rules for creating materials with pathway-dependent, 'muscle-like' mechanical responses (e.g., tunable fiber formation and viscoelasticity) that are not accessible via equilibrium processing.
proposals.novelty: While non-equilibrium studies exist for simple block copolymers, this proposal uniquely applies pathway engineering to complex, multi-component soft matter systems central to the Bates group's expertise—specifically ternary blends and bicontinuous microemulsions—linking processing history to non-ergodic structures and rheology.

proposals.title: Renormalization Group Analysis of Fluctuation Effects and Critical Exponents in Thermally Responsive Triblock Copolymer Gels
proposals.motivation: The phase behavior of complex polymer blends and gels, such as poloxamer (PEO-PPO-PEO) hydrogels and ternary blends with brush polymers, is governed by mean-field theories that fail near critical points and in confined geometries like bicontinuous microemulsions. Pre-2026, understanding the universal scaling and long-wavelength composition fluctuations in these self-assembling systems remains a significant challenge, limiting the predictive design of materials with tunable mechanical and swelling properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying renormalization group (RG) techniques from critical phenomena will quantitatively predict the shift in order-disorder transition temperatures and the scaling of correlation lengths in model triblock copolymer gels and ternary blends, capturing fluctuation effects neglected by mean-field theory.
proposals.method: We will develop a Landau-Ginzburg-Wilson Hamiltonian for the order parameter (composition) of model systems, specifically P407 (Pluronic F127) aqueous solutions and A/B/A-B ternary blends. The RG flow equations will be derived using the momentum-shell integration method, integrating out short-wavelength fluctuations to compute renormalized interaction parameters and critical exponents. This theoretical framework will be coupled with experimental data from temperature-controlled SAXS and rheology on precisely synthesized polymers to validate the predicted scaling relations near the critical point.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles theoretical framework for fluctuation-dominated phase transitions in complex, thermoresponsive polymer gels and blends. It will deliver quantitative predictions for transition temperatures and domain sizes, directly aiding the engineering of next-generation materials with precisely tuned sol-gel transitions and swelling behavior for biomedical applications.
proposals.novelty: While RG theory is established for simple binary blends and block copolymers, its application to experimentally relevant, multi-component functional materials like thermoreversible poloxamer gels and ternary blends with bottlebrush polymers is novel and bridges a significant gap between abstract statistical mechanics and applied polymer science.

proposals.title: Quantifying Interfacial Structure and Dynamics in Thermoresponsive Triblock Copolymer Gels and Blends via Colloid-Inspired Small-Angle Scattering Analysis
proposals.motivation: Understanding the microstructure of complex polymer systems like poloxamer hydrogels and ternary blends is critical for controlling properties such as mechanical strength and swelling. Pre-2026, analysis of these nanostructures via small-angle X-ray/neutron scattering (SAXS/SANS) often relies on simplified models that fail to capture interfacial details and thermal fluctuations. Advanced analysis frameworks from colloidal science, which treat particle interactions and interfaces with greater rigor, remain underutilized in polymer physics.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying scattering analysis methods developed for colloidal suspensions—specifically, the Generalized Indirect Fourier Transform (GIFT) and two-Yukawa potential fitting—to thermoresponsive triblock copolymers and blends will reveal quantitative, temperature-dependent parameters for interfacial width, bending rigidity, and undulation dynamics that are inaccessible with standard polymer models.
proposals.method: We will synthesize model P407 (PEO-PPO-PEO) hydrogels and POEGMA/PS ternary blends as studied in recent Bates group work. SAXS and SANS experiments will be conducted across a temperature range (20-60°C). Scattering data will be analyzed using the GIFT method, implemented via the IRENA package, to obtain real-space pair distance distribution functions without assuming a specific form factor. For bicontinuous and lamellar phases, we will adapt the two-Yukawa potential model, typically used for colloidal interactions, to fit the structure factor and extract effective interfacial membrane parameters like bending modulus (κ) and spontaneous curvature.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new quantitative framework for linking the molecular architecture of amphiphilic polymers and blends to their mesoscale structure and rheology. It will provide direct metrics for interfacial properties in gels and bicontinuous phases, advancing the fundamental design of soft materials for biomedical and separation applications. The methodology will bridge a gap between colloidal and polymer scattering communities.
proposals.novelty: While scattering is routine in polymer science, the explicit adaptation of colloidal interaction potential models and real-space transform techniques to analyze the interfaces in self-assembled triblock copolymer gels and ternary blends is a novel, cross-disciplinary approach. This moves beyond identifying phase diagrams to quantifying interfacial thermodynamics and dynamics.

proposals.title: Chemical and Thermal Stability of Photocrosslinked Polybutadiene Core Micelles in Complex Aqueous and Polymeric Environments
proposals.motivation: The development of cross-linked micelles, particularly with photocrosslinkable polybutadiene (PB) cores as demonstrated in Bates' recent work on bicontinuous microemulsions, promises robust nanoscale templates and delivery vehicles. However, their practical application in fields like drug delivery or as structural elements in polymer blends requires predictable stability under thermal stress and in chemically diverse environments, such as in the presence of other polymers, salts, or organic solvents. Pre-2026 literature lacks systematic studies on how the cross-link density and core chemistry of these model micelles dictate their integrity beyond initial formation conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the chemical stability of photocrosslinked PB-core micelles is primarily governed by the cross-link density within the core, while their thermal stability and dimensional integrity in blends are determined by the interplay between the glass transition of the cross-linked core and the thermodynamics of the surrounding corona and matrix.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of poly(ethylene oxide)-b-polybutadiene (PEO-PB) diblock copolymers with varying PB block lengths and incorporate photocrosslinkable moieties. Micelles with cross-linked PB cores will be prepared in aqueous solution via self-assembly and UV curing. Stability will be quantified by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and cryogenic transmission electron microscopy (cryo-TEM) before and after exposure to: 1) elevated temperatures (25-90°C), 2) varying ionic strengths and pH, and 3) ternary blends with homopolymers like polystyrene (PS) and poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), leveraging Bates' group expertise in blend analysis. Solvent extraction experiments will assess core insolubility.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish structure-property relationships linking PB core architecture (length, cross-link density) to micelle robustness, providing critical design rules for their use as nanoreactors or toughening agents in materials. It will directly inform the potential of these cross-linked nanostructures in biomedical applications by mapping their stability in physiologically relevant conditions.
proposals.novelty: While cross-linked micelles are known, a systematic investigation of photocrosslinked PB cores—a chemistry central to Bates' recent microemulsion work—across thermal, aqueous, and polymeric blend environments is unprecedented. The study uniquely connects the core's rubbery network physics to the stability of the overall nanostructure in complex media.

proposals.title: Controlling Thermogel Mechanics and Morphology via Core-Crosslinking in Poly(butadiene-b-ethylene oxide) Micelles
proposals.motivation: Thermoresponsive block copolymer hydrogels, like those based on poloxamers (PEO-PPO-PEO), are critical for biomedical applications such as drug delivery and tissue engineering scaffolds. However, their mechanical properties and morphological stability are often limited by the dynamic nature of self-assembled micelles. Pre-2026 work on photocrosslinkable bicontinuous microemulsions by Bates' group demonstrates the power of cross-linking to lock in nanostructure, but the systematic effect of cross-linking density within the core of discrete micelles on gel rheology and morphology remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increasing the cross-linking density within the hydrophobic polybutadiene (PB) core of PEO-PB-PEO triblock copolymer micelles will progressively restrict chain mobility, leading to a transition from dynamic, temperature-sensitive networks to elastic, solid-like gels with arrested morphology and enhanced mechanical integrity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of PEO-PB-PEO triblock copolymers with varying levels of pendent vinyl groups on the PB block. Micelles will be formed in aqueous solution via temperature-induced self-assembly. Core cross-linking will be achieved using UV-initiated thiol-ene 'click' chemistry, with dithiol cross-linker concentration systematically varied to control cross-link density. Morphology will be characterized by cryogenic transmission electron microscopy (cryo-TEM) and small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS). Mechanical properties will be quantified via oscillatory shear rheology to measure elastic (G') and viscous (G'') moduli as functions of temperature and cross-linking density.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative relationship between nanoscale core cross-linking and macroscale gel mechanics, providing a design principle for engineering robust, nanostructured hydrogels. It will extend the concept of cross-linked polymeric microemulsions to discrete micellar systems, offering new pathways to create injectable, yet mechanically stable, biomaterials with tunable erosion profiles.
proposals.novelty: While cross-linking of block copolymer assemblies is known, systematically varying the cross-link density specifically within the core of well-defined triblock micelles to decouple its effect on morphology from mechanics is a novel approach. This moves beyond simple gelation studies to create a library of materials with graded viscoelastic properties from a single polymer precursor.

proposals.title: Elasticity of Cross-Linked Wormlike Micelles: A Polymer Network Analogy for Triblock Copolymer Gels
proposals.motivation: Thermoreversible gels formed by Pluronic (PEO-PPO-PEO) triblock copolymers, such as P407, exhibit complex viscoelasticity crucial for biomedical applications like drug delivery and tissue engineering. While the gelation is attributed to a micellar network, the mechanical properties are often described empirically. A fundamental, predictive model linking the nanoscale structure of cross-linked wormlike micelles to macroscopic elasticity is lacking, limiting rational design of next-generation soft materials with muscle-like or fiber-like mechanical response.
proposals.hypothesis: The elastic plateau modulus of a physically cross-linked triblock copolymer gel, where micellar cores act as cross-link points and bridging midblocks form elastically active strands, can be quantitatively modeled by adapting classical rubber elasticity and phantom network theories, with strand functionality and length distribution dictated by the core corona interface.
proposals.method: We will synthesize model photocrosslinkable PEO-PPO-PEO triblocks to create permanent networks mimicking the transient micellar structure. Small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) will characterize the core cross-link spacing and strand length. The mechanical data from rheology will be analyzed using the classical affine and phantom network models (G = νkT), where the network strand density ν is derived from the micellar core aggregation number and bridging fraction. We will extend the model by incorporating a distribution of elastically active strand lengths, calculated from the known polymer architecture and core geometry, to predict the modulus.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-principles theoretical framework for predicting the elasticity of self-assembled block copolymer networks, moving beyond phenomenological fitting. It will provide direct design rules for tailoring gel stiffness in biomedical hydrogels and offer a novel perspective on the mechanics of physically associated networks by treating them as transient polymer networks with well-defined junction points.
proposals.novelty: While network theories are standard for chemically cross-linked gels, their rigorous application to the core-cross-linked, wormlike micelle architecture of Pluronic gels is novel. The approach uniquely connects the well-defined thermodynamics of block copolymer self-assembly (pre-2026 strength of the Bates group) with the continuum mechanics of rubber elasticity.

proposals.title: Kinetic Pathways and Dynamic Barriers in Order-Order Transitions of Block Polymer Melts: From Gyroid to Lamellae
proposals.motivation: The equilibrium phase behavior of block polymers is well-characterized, but the kinetic pathways and energy barriers governing transitions between complex, non-lamellar mesophases (e.g., gyroid to lamellae) remain poorly understood. This knowledge gap limits the controlled processing of these materials into non-equilibrium, defect-free nanostructures for advanced membranes and photonic materials. A predictive model for these dynamics is essential for moving beyond equilibrium structure mapping.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transition from a bicontinuous gyroid (G) to a lamellar (L) phase with increasing segregation strength proceeds via a nucleation and growth mechanism through a metastable perforated lamellar (PL) intermediate, and that the activation barrier is dominated by the chain stretching entropy penalty associated with forming the nascent lamellar domain interface.
proposals.method: We will employ dynamic self-consistent field theory (DSCFT) simulations on model A-B diblock and A-B-A triblock systems, explicitly modeling the chain dynamics via the external potential dynamics algorithm. The simulations will be initiated from a metastable gyroid configuration, and a time-dependent segregation parameter (χN) will be increased to drive the transition. We will analyze the evolving structure factor, interfacial curvature, and chain conformation to map the kinetic pathway and quantify the free energy barrier using string method calculations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed theoretical framework for the kinetics of order-order transitions between 3D network and layered phases in block polymers. It will identify the critical metastable intermediates and quantify the activation barriers, offering design rules for kinetic trapping of desired morphologies. This directly informs the processing of poloxamer-based hydrogels and photocrosslinkable bicontinuous microemulsions where such transitions are relevant.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on equilibrium phases or order-disorder kinetics, applying DSCFT and the string method to explicitly model the non-equilibrium pathway between two complex ordered phases is novel. The integration of chain-level dynamic analysis with mesoscale morphological evolution for this specific transition has not been systematically explored.

proposals.title: Topological Data Analysis of Complex Block Copolymer Phases: Quantifying Connectivity in Bicontinuous and Network Structures
proposals.motivation: The precise characterization of complex block copolymer morphologies, such as bicontinuous microemulsions and gyroids, remains a challenge. Traditional methods like scattering and microscopy provide limited quantitative insight into the three-dimensional topological connectivity, which critically dictates transport properties and mechanical response in applications like membranes and tissue engineering scaffolds. A rigorous, quantitative framework for comparing topological features across different polymer chemistries and processing conditions is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that persistent homology, a tool from topological data analysis (TDA), can robustly quantify and differentiate the topological connectivity of complex block copolymer phases, revealing correlations between molecular architecture (e.g., poloxamer or triblock design), processing, and the resulting network structure that are inaccessible to conventional analysis.
proposals.method: We will apply computational homology to 3D density fields generated from self-consistent field theory (SCFT) simulations of model A/B/A-B triblock and poloxamer systems, as well as to segmented 3D reconstructions from electron tomography of physically crosslinked samples. Using the persistent homology algorithm (specifically, computing persistence barcodes/diagrams for 0D and 1D homology features), we will extract quantitative descriptors of pore and tunnel networks. These topological metrics will be correlated with rheological data (moduli) and transport simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, quantitative language for describing the topology of soft material phases, directly linking molecular design to functional network properties. It will provide a predictive computational tool for designing block copolymer architectures that target specific topological features for advanced applications in selective filtration, drug delivery, and biomimetic scaffolds.
proposals.novelty: While TDA has been applied in materials science, its systematic application to the rich phase behavior of block copolymers, especially to analyze and compare the subtle topological differences between phases like gyroids, O70, and disordered bicontinuous states, is novel and non-obvious.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Triply Periodic Network Phases in Block Copolymer Blends via Self-Consistent Field Theory and Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Triply periodic minimal surfaces (e.g., gyroid, diamond, primitive) formed by block copolymers offer exceptional potential for photonic, filtration, and tissue engineering applications. However, discovering the precise polymer parameters (e.g., composition, interaction parameter, architecture) that stabilize a specific target network remains a challenging, trial-and-error process, limiting the rational design of these complex materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by coupling self-consistent field theory (SCFT) calculations with a machine learning-based surrogate model, we can efficiently map the high-dimensional parameter space of block copolymer blends to their equilibrium morphologies, enabling the inverse solution for target triply periodic structures.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse design pipeline using a pre-2026 framework. First, we will generate a large, high-fidelity dataset of equilibrium structures by performing SCFT calculations for a diverse range of A/B/A-B ternary blend parameters, inspired by Bates' recent work. This dataset will train a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) to learn the mapping from a target structure (represented as a density field) to the polymer parameter set. The cGAN's predictions will be validated and refined through iterative SCFT calculations, ensuring thermodynamic stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a computational tool for the *a priori* design of block copolymer blends that yield desired triply periodic network phases. It will establish a quantitative relationship between blend parameters and complex 3D morphologies, moving beyond phenomenological rules. The methodology will be a blueprint for inverse design in soft matter.
proposals.novelty: While SCFT forward modeling is standard, applying a cGAN to solve the inverse problem for ternary blends targeting specific network phases is novel. The approach integrates Bates' expertise in complex blends and poloxamer/bicontinuous systems with emerging pre-2026 ML strategies for materials discovery.

proposals.title: Controlled Shear-Induced Disordering of Self-Assembled Block Copolymer Gels Using Dynamical Systems Control Principles
proposals.motivation: Shear is traditionally used to align and order soft materials like block copolymer gels and membranes. However, the deliberate use of shear as a tool to *disrupt* equilibrium order and access novel, sustained non-equilibrium states remains underexplored. This research addresses a fundamental gap in non-equilibrium materials science by asking if targeted shear perturbations can act as a control parameter to navigate between distinct material states, analogous to driving a dynamical system between attractors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying precisely timed, high-amplitude shear pulses or oscillatory profiles, informed by control theory concepts, can destabilize the equilibrium ordered structure of self-assembled block copolymer hydrogels (e.g., P407/poloxamer systems) and drive them into distinct, metastable disordered or re-configured states that are not accessible via equilibrium processing.
proposals.method: We will synthesize well-defined photocrosslinkable triblock copolymers (e.g., POEGMA-PS-POEGMA) and poloxamer (P407) gels to create model systems with tunable equilibrium order (gels, bicontinuous microemulsions). Using a rheometer with precise temporal control, we will apply designed shear forcing protocols—including high-rate step strains, oscillatory shear with programmed amplitude modulation, and chaotic mixing profiles—as the 'control input'. The structural response will be characterized in situ via rheo-small-angle X-ray scattering (rheo-SAXS) and ex situ via electron microscopy on photocrosslinked samples, mapping the shear-forcing parameter space (rate, amplitude, waveform) to the resulting material state.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new framework for using shear as a deliberate disordering tool, generating a 'control diagram' for non-equilibrium material states. It will provide fundamental insights into the nonlinear dynamics of self-assembled polymers under forcing and could lead to new processing routes for creating materials with tailored, history-dependent properties.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the conventional role of shear from an ordering to a disordering agent and explicitly applying concepts from dynamical systems control theory (e.g., forcing between attractors) to materials design, a conceptual crossover not previously explored in polymer science.

proposals.title: Decoupling Equilibrium and Fluctuation Contributions in Sheared Block Copolymer Melts via In-situ Rheo-SAXS
proposals.motivation: Understanding the structure-property relationship in self-assembled block copolymers under flow is critical for processing materials with tailored mechanical properties, such as thermoplastic elastomers and structured gels. Pre-2026 work, including studies on poloxamer hydrogels and ternary blends, has established that complex morphologies can be described by a superposition of an equilibrium ordered structure and composition fluctuations, but this model lacks direct experimental validation under dynamic shear and temperature conditions. This gap limits our ability to predict and control the viscoelastic response of these materials during fabrication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the scattering signature of a sheared, compositionally fluctuating block copolymer melt can be quantitatively decomposed into distinct components arising from the equilibrium lamellar morphology and from thermally driven composition fluctuations, and that the intensity and orientation of each component will scale predictably with shear rate and temperature according to the superposition model.
proposals.method: We will employ in-situ rheo-small-angle X-ray scattering (rheo-SAXS) with a temperature-controlled Couette cell. A model poly(styrene-b-isoprene) diblock or poly(ethylene oxide-b-propylene oxide-b-ethylene oxide) (PEO-PPO-PEO) triblock system near the order-disorder transition will be subjected to controlled oscillatory and steady shear. 2D SAXS patterns will be acquired simultaneously with rheological data. A custom analysis algorithm will fit the scattering patterns to a superposition model, separating the sharp Bragg peaks (equilibrium lamellae) from a diffuse, anisotropic scattering halo (fluctuations) to extract the intensity, orientation, and correlation length of each component as a function of shear rate and temperature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative validation of the superposition model for block copolymer melts under shear, directly linking specific structural motifs to macroscopic viscoelasticity. It will establish a foundational framework for interpreting the non-equilibrium dynamics of complex polymer blends and gels, enabling more rational design of processing protocols for structured soft materials.
proposals.novelty: While rheo-SAXS is established, its application to rigorously decouple and track the simultaneous evolution of equilibrium and fluctuating structural components in a dynamically sheared melt is novel. This moves beyond characterizing average structure to probe the interplay between equilibrium order and non-equilibrium processes in real time.

proposals.title: 4D In Situ Tomography of Shear-Induced Alignment Pathways in Poloxamer Triblock Copolymers
proposals.motivation: Shear-induced alignment of block copolymers is critical for processing nanostructured materials with anisotropic properties, such as muscle-mimetic fibers or templated membranes. However, the kinetic pathway from a disordered or poorly ordered state to a long-range aligned structure remains poorly understood in three dimensions, as conventional scattering techniques provide only ensemble-averaged, low-resolution data. Capturing the real-space, 3D evolution of domains under shear is essential to distinguish between the growth of aligned grains and the dissolution of transient defects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that shear-induced long-range ordering in symmetric triblock copolymers proceeds via the nucleation and anisotropic growth of aligned grains, while transient, fluctuating micellar domains act as a reservoir, dissolving to supply material for grain growth rather than merging coherently.
proposals.method: We will integrate a miniaturized, TEM-compatible shear stage with precise temperature control to perform in situ rheology on thin films of model PEO-PPO-PEO (Poloxamer) triblock copolymers. Sequential tilt-series tomography will be acquired at controlled time intervals during the application of a steady shear rate, generating a 4D (3D+time) dataset. Image analysis will employ segmentation algorithms (e.g., watershed, machine learning-based) to track individual domain boundaries over time, quantifying metrics like grain size, orientation order parameter, and defect density as a function of local shear strain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, 3D visualization of the kinetic pathway for shear-induced block copolymer alignment, establishing a mechanistic link between local shear conditions and microstructural evolution. It will yield quantitative metrics for grain growth and defect annihilation kinetics, creating a foundational dataset to validate and improve time-dependent theories of polymer ordering under flow.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of in situ TEM tomography with controlled shear rheometry enables direct, time-resolved 3D observation of ordering kinetics, moving beyond indirect scattering methods or 2D projections, to uniquely resolve the fate of coexisting transient and stable microstructures.

proposals.title: Kinetic Arrest and Structure in Quenched Polymer Melts: Mapping the Role of Engineered Polydispersity
proposals.motivation: The pursuit of equilibrium self-assembly in block polymers has long emphasized monodispersity for structural perfection. However, many functional materials, from tough gels to impact-resistant glasses, derive properties from non-equilibrium, kinetically arrested states. Pre-2026 work on poloxamer hydrogels and ternary blends demonstrates the profound influence of composition and architecture on phase behavior and rheology, yet the deliberate use of polydispersity as a kinetic control parameter in rapid quenching processes remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the shape and breadth of the molecular weight distribution, not just the average, will dictate the kinetic pathway and final arrested morphology (e.g., bicontinuous, glassy, or gel-like) upon deep thermal quenching, enabling the encoding of functional properties through designed polydispersity and processing history.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of model AB diblock and ABA triblock polymers (e.g., PEO-PPO-based) with controlled, non-uniform molecular weight distributions using tailored anionic polymerization and blending techniques. These melts will be subjected to rapid temperature quenches from a disordered state using a custom stage, with quenching rates and depths systematically varied. The arrested structures will be characterized via in-situ X-ray scattering, rheology, and electron microscopy, correlating the polydispersity profile and quench protocol to the resulting non-equilibrium morphology and mechanical properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative framework linking engineered polydispersity to non-equilibrium assembly pathways, moving beyond its traditional role as a nuisance. It will provide design rules for creating isotropic materials with tailored mechanical response (e.g., muscle-like fibers or energy-dissipating networks) directly from melt processing, expanding the toolbox for functional polymer materials.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in treating polydispersity not as a defect but as a primary, programmable variable to steer kinetics, challenging the monodispersity paradigm. It uniquely combines precise polymer synthesis, extreme processing (quenching), and the study of arrested states like glasses and gels, a fusion beyond typical equilibrium self-assembly studies.

proposals.title: Sequence-Encoded Interaction Potentials: Mapping Fluctuation Spectra in DNA-Functionalized Block Copolymer Melts
proposals.motivation: The phase behavior and dynamics of block copolymers are governed by the Flory-Huggins interaction parameter (χ). However, χ is typically a fixed, chemistry-dependent value, limiting our ability to independently tune interaction strength and polymer architecture. The advent of programmable nucleic acid hybridization offers a route to create sequence-defined, tunable, and specific interactions within synthetic polymers, providing a novel handle to manipulate fluctuation landscapes and phase transitions in soft materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sequence-specific binding energy of tethered single-stranded DNA can act as a programmable, additive contribution to the effective χ parameter, allowing the fluctuation spectra and order-disorder transition temperatures of block copolymer melts to be universally controlled by nucleic acid sequence design, independent of the underlying polymer backbone chemistry.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of symmetric polystyrene-b-poly(ethylene oxide) (PS-PEO) diblock copolymers, functionalizing the PEO block terminus with single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) of controlled length and sequence. Using a combination of temperature-controlled small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and rheology, we will measure the fluctuation spectra and order-disorder transition (ODT) for blends of complementary and non-complementary DNA-functionalized chains. We will systematically vary the DNA sequence length (8-20 bases) and binding strength (via GC content and mismatches) to map their quantitative effect on the ODT and the structure factor S(q) in the disordered state, comparing results to self-consistent field theory (SCFT) calculations modified to include a sequence-dependent binding free energy term.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative relationship between nucleic acid sequence design and the fluctuation thermodynamics of block copolymer melts. It will provide a new paradigm for 'fluctuation engineering,' enabling the creation of materials with precisely tailored transition temperatures and dynamic responsiveness. The findings will bridge the fields of polymer physics and DNA nanotechnology, offering design rules for smart, programmable soft matter.
proposals.novelty: While DNA has been used to direct nanoparticle assembly, its application as a programmable, *in-situ* tunable interaction parameter within a homogenous block copolymer melt to directly control fluctuation spectra and the ODT is unexplored. This moves beyond using DNA as a static 'sticker' and instead treats it as a dynamic, sequence-defined thermodynamic variable.

proposals.title: Generalizing Composition-Dependent Fluctuations in Block Copolymer Melts: A Systematic Study of Non-Symmetric and Chemically Distinct Systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies of composition fluctuations in block copolymer melts have largely focused on symmetric or near-symmetric diblock compositions, often using model systems like poly(styrene-b-isoprene) or poly(styrene-b-methyl methacrylate). The Bates group's recent work on ternary blends and bicontinuous microemulsions highlights the critical role of composition and interfacial activity in controlling structure and rheology. Extending fluctuation analysis to a broader range of volume fractions and monomer chemistries is essential to develop a universal framework for predicting the stability and self-assembly kinetics of complex block copolymer formulations, which are foundational for advanced materials like thermoplastic elastomers and nanostructured membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the enhanced composition fluctuations observed near the order-disorder transition in symmetric diblock copolymers are a general phenomenon, but that the fluctuation amplitude, correlation length, and kinetic pathways will depend systematically on block asymmetry (volume fraction, f) and the thermodynamic interaction parameter (χ) of the monomer pair.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of compositionally asymmetric diblock copolymers (e.g., polystyrene-b-polyisoprene, PS-PI, with f_PI from 0.2 to 0.8) and chemically distinct triblock copolymers (e.g., poloxamer-like PEO-PPO-PEO and model ABA triblocks). Fluctuation behavior will be characterized using a combination of temperature-dependent small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) to measure the scattering peak intensity and width, and dynamic rheology to probe the viscoelastic signature of fluctuations near the ODT. The experimental data will be analyzed using the Fredrickson-Helfand theory for fluctuations and compared to self-consistent field theory (SCFT) simulations for asymmetric compositions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a comprehensive experimental dataset mapping fluctuation regimes across composition and chemistry space for block copolymers. It will provide critical validation for theoretical models beyond the symmetric limit and offer practical guidelines for tuning processing conditions (e.g., temperature, shear) to manipulate pre-transitional structures in technologically relevant, non-ideal block copolymer systems.
proposals.novelty: While fluctuations are well-studied in symmetric diblocks, a systematic experimental investigation targeting highly asymmetric and triblock architectures, particularly those with chemically distinct mid-blocks (like PPO in poloxamers), is lacking. This bridges fundamental fluctuation physics with the complex compositions used in applied hydrogel and blend research.

proposals.title: Active Pathway Engineering of Metastable Bicontinuous Phases in Triblock Copolymer Melts via In-situ Rheo-SAXS
proposals.motivation: The self-assembly of block polymers is a powerful route to nanostructured materials, but the final structure is often limited to equilibrium phases. Accessing long-lived, non-equilibrium morphologies like metastable bicontinuous networks could yield materials with unique transport and mechanical properties, akin to biological tissues or synthetic muscles. Current methods lack the ability to actively steer rapid kinetic pathways in real-time based on direct structural feedback, limiting control over these complex processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying precisely defined shear and thermal histories, guided by real-time small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) structural data, will enable the deliberate navigation of the energy landscape in triblock copolymer melts to trap non-equilibrium, bicontinuous network structures that are otherwise inaccessible.
proposals.method: We will employ an in-situ rheo-SAXS setup with precise temperature control to study model poly(ethylene-alt-propylene)-b-poly(ethylene oxide) (PEP-PEO-PEP) triblock copolymers and poloxamer (Pluronic) analogues. A custom feedback algorithm will be developed to analyze the evolving SAXS patterns (e.g., peak positions, intensities, azimuthal anisotropy) and dynamically adjust the applied shear rate or temperature ramp to guide the assembly pathway. Specific shear protocols (oscillatory vs. steady, step-shear sequences) will be tested to disrupt the formation of equilibrium lamellar or cylindrical phases and promote the kinetic arrest of a disordered, sponge-like or gyroid precursor.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a methodology for active kinetic pathway engineering in soft matter, moving beyond passive observation. It will produce a framework for designing shear and thermal histories to access targeted metastable states, and deliver new structure-property relationships for non-equilibrium bicontinuous triblock copolymer materials with potential applications as tunable membranes or synthetic scaffolds.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, feedback-controlled integration of rheology and SAXS to *engineer* pathways in real-time, rather than merely observing them, and in targeting the deliberate trapping of complex bicontinuous morphologies as metastable states in simple linear triblock systems.

proposals.title: Decoding Architectural Fingerprints: A Machine Learning Framework for Predicting Fluctuation Phenomena in Multiblock Copolymers
proposals.motivation: Fluctuation phenomena, such as shifts in the order-disorder transition (ODT) and interfacial broadening, critically influence the thermal stability and mechanical properties of block copolymer morphologies. While well-studied in simple diblocks, the impact of architectural complexity—sequence, asymmetry, and number of blocks in triblocks and multiblocks—on these fluctuations remains a vast, underexplored design space. A predictive framework is needed to move beyond trial-and-error and harness fluctuations for tailoring material response, such as in thermally-responsive gels and tough, nanostructured blends.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models can learn the non-linear mapping between multiblock copolymer architectural parameters and quantitative metrics of fluctuation effects, enabling the inverse design of polymers with targeted fluctuation-stabilized or suppressed morphologies.
proposals.method: We will generate a large dataset using high-throughput, coarse-grained molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of A/B-type multiblock copolymers, varying block sequence, compositional asymmetry (f), and total number of blocks (N). Simulation observables will include the ODT temperature shift (χN_ODT) relative to mean-field and interfacial width. This data will train supervised ML models, including graph neural networks (to encode chain connectivity) and gradient-boosted trees, to predict fluctuation metrics. The trained model will then be used for inverse design via Bayesian optimization, specifying target ODT or interfacial properties to propose candidate architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative, data-driven link between complex multiblock architecture and fluctuation physics, creating a design tool for polymers with tailored thermal and mechanical responses. It will accelerate the discovery of new materials for applications like tunable poloxamer-based hydrogels and nanostructured thermoplastic elastomers with enhanced toughness.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to polymer discovery, its integration with coarse-grained simulation specifically to decode the 'fluctuation fingerprint' of multiblock sequences is a novel synthesis of soft-matter physics and data science. This moves beyond equilibrium phase prediction to target the physics governing stability and interfacial properties.

proposals.title: Field-Guided Kinetic Trapping of DNA-Functionalized Block Polymers into Metastable Nanostructures
proposals.motivation: The self-assembly of block polymers (BPs) is a powerful route to nanostructured materials, but equilibrium structures are limited by thermodynamics. Conversely, DNA origami offers exquisite programmability but lacks the robust mechanical properties of bulk polymers. Merging these fields could yield materials with unprecedented complexity, but achieving non-equilibrium, kinetically trapped nanostructures with DNA-programmed specificity remains a significant challenge. This work addresses the inverse problem of quenching—actively guiding the kinetic pathway to a desired metastable state—which is crucial for creating functional materials with properties not found at equilibrium.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a tunable external field (electric or shear) during a rapid thermal quench of DNA-handle-functionalized block polymers will bias the kinetic pathways of DNA-mediated association, enabling the deliberate trapping of complex, metastable nanostructures that are programmed by both DNA sequence and field parameters.
proposals.method: We will synthesize poly(ethylene oxide)-block-poly(styrene) (PEO-b-PS) and poly(ethylene oxide)-block-poly(methyl methacrylate) (PEO-b-PMMA) diblock polymers, functionalizing the PEO block terminus with single-stranded DNA. These will be blended with complementary DNA-grafted homopolymers or surfactants to modulate interactions. Samples will be heated to a disordered state and then subjected to a rapid quench under applied electric fields (using in-situ SAXS cells with electrodes) or controlled shear in a Linkam shear stage. The phase behavior and nanostructure evolution will be characterized via temperature-resolved small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and rheology, correlating field strength/quench rate with final non-equilibrium morphology.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new methodology for creating non-equilibrium polymer nanostructures with addressable, specific interactions, directly merging the fields of polymer physics and DNA nanotechnology. It will provide fundamental insights into the kinetic pathways of field-directed self-assembly in complex, interacting systems, and deliver design rules for metastable nanomaterials with potential applications in photonics, membranes, and templated synthesis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the active, field-guided control of the quenching process for DNA-programmed block polymers, moving beyond passive thermal quenching or equilibrium DNA hybridization to solve the kinetic trapping problem and access a new class of metastable, addressable nanostructures.

proposals.title: Theory-Guided Amplification and Suppression of Compositional Fluctuations in Ternary Polymer Blends
proposals.motivation: The fluctuation spectrum of a polymer blend critically influences its phase behavior, dynamics, and material properties, yet direct, rational control over these fluctuations remains a challenge. Pre-2026 work has shown that adding a third component, such as a homopolymer or copolymer, can dramatically alter phase diagrams and interfacial properties, but a predictive framework linking blend architecture to specific fluctuation signatures is lacking. This gap limits the ability to engineer materials with tailored stability, transport, or optical responses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the structure factor and phase behavior of a model block copolymer system can be predictively and reversibly tuned by the strategic addition of a third polymeric component, enabling targeted amplification or suppression of composition fluctuations at specific wavevectors.
proposals.method: We will employ self-consistent field theory (SCFT) and polymer reference interaction site model (PRISM) theory to computationally screen ternary blends of a model poly(styrene-b-isoprene) diblock copolymer with either a polystyrene homopolymer or a poly(styrene-b-ethylene oxide) diblock. The calculations will map the effect of composition and molecular weight on the structure factor S(q) and phase boundaries. Promising theoretical predictions will be validated by synthesizing precisely defined polymers via anionic polymerization, preparing ternary blends, and characterizing fluctuation spectra using temperature-controlled small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a closed-loop, theory-guided platform for designing polymer blends with prescribed fluctuation spectra. It will provide fundamental insights into how copolymer architecture modulates collective composition modes in complex mixtures. The resulting design rules will advance the field of polymer thermodynamics and enable new strategies for controlling self-assembly kinetics and material properties.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined ternary blend phase behavior, the direct, theory-guided targeting of specific features in the fluctuation spectrum (beyond simply shifting the order-disorder transition) represents a novel and non-obvious objective. The integration of SCFT and PRISM for this predictive task, coupled with targeted SAXS validation, moves beyond phenomenological observation to a genuine design principle.

proposals.title: Controlling Fluctuation Spectra and Phase Behavior in Ternary Blends of Block Copolymers with Added Homopolymers
proposals.motivation: The phase behavior and fluctuation spectra of block copolymer melts are central to their self-assembly and material properties, yet precise control over these fluctuations in complex, multi-component systems remains a challenge. Pre-2026 work by Bates and others has established the profound impact of composition and architecture on ternary blend morphology, but a systematic study linking added homopolymer characteristics to the fluctuation spectra of a primary block copolymer matrix is lacking. Understanding this link is crucial for designing materials with tunable viscoelastic and mechanical properties, relevant to applications like thermoplastic elastomers and nanostructured membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adding a compositionally symmetric homopolymer to a lamellae-forming diblock copolymer matrix will selectively modify the fluctuation spectra of the blend, either suppressing or enhancing composition fluctuations depending on the homopolymer's molecular weight and interaction parameter, thereby offering a novel route to control order-disorder transitions and rheological response.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of model poly(styrene-b-isoprene) (SI) diblock copolymers with varying molecular weights near the order-disorder transition (ODT). These will be blended with narrow-distribution polystyrene (PS) homopolymers of varying molecular weights (both lower and higher than the PS block). Phase behavior will be characterized using temperature-dependent small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and dynamic rheology to map ODTs and fluctuation regimes. The fluctuation spectra will be quantified by analyzing the SAXS peak intensity and line shape as a function of temperature and blend composition, building on the Fredrickson-Helfand theory for block copolymer melts.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish quantitative design rules for using homopolymer additives as 'fluctuation modifiers' in block copolymer systems, directly linking blend formulation to thermodynamic and dynamic properties. It will provide a new framework for tuning the processing window and final nanostructure of block copolymer-based materials without altering the copolymer chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While homopolymer addition to block copolymers is well-studied for morphology control, the explicit targeting and systematic measurement of the fluctuation spectrum itself as the primary experimental variable is a novel approach. This moves beyond equilibrium phase diagrams to actively engineer the pre-transitional behavior critical for processing and dynamic property control.

proposals.title: Phase-Field Modeling of Non-Equilibrium Self-Assembly Pathways in Multiblock Copolymers: From Processing to Morphology
proposals.motivation: The self-assembly of multiblock polymers, such as poloxamers and triblocks, into complex nanostructures like bicontinuous microemulsions is central to creating advanced materials for biomedicine and membranes. However, the kinetic pathways during processing (e.g., solvent casting, thermal quenching, photocrosslinking) are poorly understood, limiting our ability to control final morphology and properties. Existing self-consistent field theory (SCFT) models primarily predict equilibrium states, failing to capture the dynamic, non-equilibrium transitions critical to manufacturing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the Cahn-Hilliard and Allen-Cahn equations from metallurgical phase-field modeling will successfully simulate the complex kinetic pathways of multiblock polymer self-assembly under processing conditions, revealing how transient structures evolve into non-equilibrium morphologies.
proposals.method: We will develop a phase-field model where conserved (Cahn-Hilliard) and non-conserved (Allen-Cahn) order parameters describe the local composition and orientation of polymer domains. The model will incorporate Flory-Huggins interaction parameters, chain architecture (A/B/A-B triblocks), and external fields representing temperature quenches and solvent evaporation. Numerical simulations will be performed using a semi-implicit Fourier spectral method to solve the time-dependent equations, mapping kinetic pathways from homogeneous blends to ordered or trapped microstructures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational framework to predict processing-structure relationships for multiblock polymers, directly linking fabrication conditions to non-equilibrium morphologies. It will offer design rules for targeting specific complex phases, like bicontinuous networks, in hydrogels and blends, advancing the engineering of functional polymeric materials.
proposals.novelty: While phase-field models are mature in metallurgy, their application to the detailed kinetic self-assembly of architecturally complex multiblock polymers, explicitly connecting to experimental processing variables like reverse poloxamer addition or photocrosslinking, is novel and represents a significant methodological crossover.

proposals.title: Phase-Field Modeling of Non-Equilibrium Microstructure Evolution in Thermoresponsive Triblock Copolymer Gels and Blends
proposals.motivation: Predicting and controlling the non-equilibrium microstructure of block copolymers during processes like gelation, solvent casting, or thermal cycling is critical for tailoring mechanical properties, such as those mimicking muscle fibers, and transport properties in membranes. Current self-consistent field theory (SCFT) excels at equilibrium structures but struggles with dynamic pathways. Phase-field models, proven for alloy solidification and polymer crystallization, offer a powerful, underexplored framework for capturing these complex kinetic evolutions in soft matter.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Cahn-Hilliard-type phase-field model, parameterized with Flory-Huggins interaction parameters and polymer dynamics, can accurately simulate the kinetic pathways of microstructure formation in poloxamer (PEO-PPO-PEO) hydrogels and A/B/A-B ternary blends, capturing effects of thermal history and composition gradients.
proposals.method: We will adapt a multi-order-parameter phase-field model, using a conserved order parameter for composition and a non-conserved parameter for gel network formation. The free energy functional will combine Flory-Huggins mixing, elastic contributions from crosslinking (for gels), and gradient penalties. Dynamics will be governed by Model B (Cahn-Hilliard) and Model A (Allen-Cahn) equations. Simulations will be performed using the FiPy finite volume PDE solver, explicitly modeling reverse poloxamer addition and ternary blend phase separation under thermal quenches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational tool for predicting time-dependent morphologies in complex polymer systems, directly linking processing conditions to final structure. It will offer fundamental insights into the kinetic trapping of non-equilibrium phases in photocrosslinkable microemulsions and ternary blends, enabling the rational design of materials with targeted rheological and swelling properties.
proposals.novelty: While phase-field modeling is standard in metallurgy, its application to the dynamic self-assembly of triblock copolymers in gels and ternary blends, explicitly connecting to experimental systems like P407 and POEGMA brushes, is novel and bridges a significant gap between hard and soft matter simulation communities.

proposals.title: Colloidal Crystallization Principles as a Guide for Ordering in Thermoplastic Elastomer Triblock Copolymer Melts and Gels
proposals.motivation: The directed self-assembly of block copolymers into long-range ordered structures is critical for advanced materials, but achieving defect-free, single-crystal-like order in bulk and gel states remains challenging. Pre-2026 colloidal science has established precise principles for controlling crystal nucleation, growth, and stability via tunable inter-particle potentials, yet these concepts are underexploited in block copolymer assembly, particularly for complex, thermoresponsive systems like poloxamer-based hydrogels and thermoplastic elastomers.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating the microphase-separated domains in triblock copolymer melts and gels as 'soft colloids' with tunable interaction potentials, principles of colloidal crystallization—such as depletion forces, controlled nucleation, and epitaxial matching—can be applied to direct their assembly into large-grained, hierarchically structured materials with enhanced mechanical properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of poly(ethylene oxide)-poly(propylene oxide)-poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO-PPO-PEO) triblock copolymers and related photocrosslinkable analogues, systematically varying the PEO block length (brush length) to modulate domain interaction potentials. Using temperature and composition as control parameters, we will employ a reverse poloxamer addition protocol to create well-defined 'soft colloidal' dispersions of micelles. We will then apply techniques from colloidal crystal engineering, including controlled evaporation/densification and template-directed epitaxial growth, to guide the assembly of these micelles into body-centered cubic or Frank-Kasper phases, characterizing structure via SAXS and rheological properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new conceptual framework for block copolymer processing by directly importing design rules from colloidal science. It will yield novel processing pathways to create large-domain, defect-controlled thermoplastic elastomer and hydrogel materials with tailored viscoelasticity, potentially mimicking the hierarchical structure and toughness of natural muscle fibers.
proposals.novelty: While block copolymer self-assembly is well-studied, the explicit treatment of microphase-separated domains as colloidal particles with engineered interactions to direct their crystallization in bulk and gel states is a novel synthesis of two mature fields. The application of these principles to dynamically assembling, temperature-sensitive poloxamer systems to control rheology is particularly unexplored.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Stability of Triblock Copolymer Polymersomes in Serum: Correlating Membrane Mechanics with Protein Corona Formation
proposals.motivation: Polymersomes, self-assembled from amphiphilic block copolymers, are promising nanocarriers for drug delivery, but their clinical translation is hindered by instability in complex biological fluids like serum. Pre-2026 work has largely focused on polymersome formation and encapsulation, with insufficient quantitative understanding of how serum proteins interact with and compromise the membrane's mechanical and chemical integrity over time. This gap limits the rational design of robust delivery systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the stability of polymersomes in serum is governed by a critical interplay between the membrane's intrinsic mechanical properties (modulus, toughness) and the composition/kinetics of the adsorbed protein corona, where specific protein interactions catalyze membrane destabilization.
proposals.method: We will fabricate polymersomes from a library of photocrosslinkable poly(ethylene oxide)-b-polybutadiene-b-poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO-PB-PEO) triblock copolymers, leveraging Bates group expertise in polymeric bicontinuous microemulsions and triblock systems to tune membrane thickness and packing. Membrane mechanical properties will be quantified via micropipette aspiration. Stability will be assessed by incubating polymersomes in fetal bovine serum and monitoring size (DLS), membrane integrity (fluorescence leakage), and protein corona composition (LC-MS/MS) over 24 hours. Correlations between initial modulus, corona evolution, and failure time will be established.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative framework linking polymersome membrane mechanics to biological fate, moving beyond simple stability assays. It will yield design rules for serum-stable nanocarriers and provide fundamental insights into how synthetic membranes interact with complex protein mixtures, a key challenge in bionanotechnology.
proposals.novelty: While protein corona analysis is common for nanoparticles, the novelty lies in directly correlating dynamic corona formation with real-time mechanical degradation of the polymersome membrane itself, using a tunable, crosslinkable triblock platform to systematically vary initial mechanical properties.

proposals.title: Controlling the Inverse Morphology Transition in Poloxamer Triblock Copolymers: From Spherical Micelles to Rod-like Structures via Temperature and Composition
proposals.motivation: The self-assembly of amphiphilic block copolymers like poloxamers (PEO-PPO-PEO) into spherical micelles is well-established, forming the basis for thermoreversible hydrogels. However, the inverse problem—systematically inducing a transition from these spherical micelles to anisotropic, rod-like structures within the same copolymer system—remains underexplored. Mastering this morphological duality is critical for advanced applications, such as mimicking the hierarchical structure of muscle fibers or creating templated, anisotropic soft materials with directionally dependent properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by precisely manipulating the temperature and the composition of a ternary blend containing a poloxamer, a hydrophobic polymer, and a selective solvent, we can drive a controlled morphological transition from spherical micelles to elongated rods. This transition will be governed by the interplay between core swelling, corona stretching, and interfacial curvature.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of photocrosslinkable poloxamer triblock copolymers with varying PPO/PEO block ratios. These will be blended with polystyrene (PS) and a solvent selective for PEO (e.g., oligo(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate-based homopolymer) to form ternary blends. Using temperature-controlled small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and cryogenic transmission electron microscopy (cryo-TEM), we will map the phase behavior and characterize the spherical-to-rod transition. Selected rod-like structures will be locked-in via UV-induced photocrosslinking of the PEO corona, enabling detailed analysis of their stability and rheological properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust experimental pathway to access and stabilize inverse, anisotropic micellar structures from conventional spherical micelle-forming copolymers. It will provide a fundamental understanding of the parameters controlling curvature inversion in soft self-assembly. The resulting methodology will offer a new platform for engineering biomimetic, fiber-like scaffolds and templated nanomaterials with controlled anisotropy.
proposals.novelty: While reverse poloxamer addition and ternary blends have been used to modify hydrogel structure, applying these concepts to deliberately invert micellar curvature and stabilize non-spherical micelles in a single copolymer system is a novel and non-obvious extension. The integration of photocrosslinking to 'freeze' transient rod-like morphologies for detailed study is a key innovative step.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Assembly of Photocrosslinked Polymersomes into Macroscopic, Anisotropic Hydrogels Mimicking Muscle Fiber Architecture
proposals.motivation: Polymersomes offer exquisite compartmentalization for drug delivery and synthetic biology, but their utility is confined to the microscopic scale. Pre-2026 work lacks robust methods to assemble these robust vesicles into bulk materials with controlled macroscopic structure and mechanical properties. Creating such materials would bridge the gap between nanoscale vesicle function and macroscale applications in tissue engineering and soft robotics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that blending photocrosslinkable amphiphilic triblock copolymers, capable of forming bicontinuous microemulsions or polymersomes, with temperature-responsive poloxamers like P407 will yield a processable precursor that can be shear-aligned and subsequently photo-cured to lock in a polymersome-based, anisotropic gel network.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of photocrosslinkable poly(ethylene oxide)-b-poly(butadiene)-b-poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO-PB-PEO) triblocks, varying hydrophobic block length to control membrane properties. These will be blended with P407 poloxamer in aqueous solution. Using pre-2026 knowledge of reverse poloxamer addition and ternary blend phase behavior, we will identify compositions that form polymersomes within a thermoreversible P407 gel matrix. This composite will be extruded or subjected to Couette flow at the gelation temperature to align the vesicles, followed by UV irradiation to crosslink the polymersome membranes, creating a permanent, aligned scaffold after subsequent removal of the P407.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable platform for fabricating bulk materials from polymersomes, providing the first direct link between vesicle membrane design and macroscopic material rheology and anisotropy. It will contribute new knowledge on the phase behavior and processability of complex polymer/vesicle blends, enabling future biomimetic materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of temperature-triggered processing (using poloxamer gels) and photo-triggered locking (of polymersomes) to create permanent, oriented macroscopic structures from vesicles, an approach not yet demonstrated in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Programming Self-Assembly Pathways in Block Copolymer Gels via Synthetically Encoded Dispersity Profiles
proposals.motivation: Polymer dispersity (Đ) profoundly impacts self-assembly and material properties, yet it is typically treated as a single, uncontrolled metric. In block copolymer systems like poloxamers, which form thermoresponsive gels and membranes, the detailed shape of the molecular weight distribution—bimodal, skewed, or multimodal—is hypothesized to be a critical, unexplored design parameter. Establishing causal links between synthetically programmed distribution profiles and emergent properties would transform dispersity from a flaw into a programmable material variable.
proposals.hypothesis: The precise shape of the chain length distribution in amphiphilic block copolymers (e.g., PEO-PPO-PEO) can be synthetically encoded to direct self-assembly pathways, dictating the resulting nanostructure, gelation temperature, and mechanical properties such as those resembling muscle fiber elasticity.
proposals.method: We will employ iterative growth techniques, such as chain shuttling polymerization or controlled coupling of discrete oligomers, to synthesize libraries of poloxamer-like triblock copolymers with designed dispersity profiles (e.g., narrow, bimodal, skewed). These polymers will be characterized via advanced SEC-MALS and used in ternary blends with homopolymers to study phase behavior. Self-assembly into gels and bicontinuous microemulsions will be probed using temperature-dependent rheology and SAXS, correlating distribution shape to nanostructure and mechanical response.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative framework linking programmed dispersity profiles to block copolymer self-assembly and rheology. It will provide synthetic strategies to tune thermogel properties for biomedical applications and deepen fundamental understanding of polydispersity in soft matter phase behavior.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond Đ as a scalar impurity metric to treat the full distribution as a synthetically programmable, high-dimensional parameter for directing self-assembly is a paradigm shift. Systematically testing this in complex, thermoresponsive triblock systems relevant to gels and blends integrates Bates group expertise in a novel way.

proposals.title: Exploiting Controlled Polydispersity in Triblock Copolymers to Engineer Hierarchical, Muscle-Mimetic Fiber Bundles
