proposals.motivation: The field of polymer self-assembly has long prioritized narrow dispersity (Đ) to achieve well-defined nanostructures. However, biological materials like muscle fibers exhibit hierarchical order and complex rheology derived from inherent polydispersity in their constituent proteins. This project challenges the dogma that low Đ is universally necessary, proposing that controlled polydispersity can be a powerful design parameter for creating advanced soft materials with emergent mechanical properties.
proposals.hypothesis: Introducing controlled, asymmetric dispersity into the end-blocks of poly(ethylene oxide)-poly(propylene oxide)-poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO-PPO-PEO) triblock copolymers will drive the formation of hierarchically structured, physically crosslinked hydrogels with tunable, muscle-like viscoelasticity and fiber bundle morphology, surpassing the property space accessible with monodisperse analogs.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PEO-PPO-PEO triblocks via anionic polymerization, systematically varying the dispersity (Đ) of the PEO end-blocks while keeping the PPO mid-block relatively monodisperse. Hydrogels will be formed via reverse poloxamer addition. Structure will be characterized using temperature-dependent SAXS and cryo-TEM to map phase behavior and morphology. Rheological properties will be quantified to establish links between Đ, hierarchical self-assembly (e.g., from micelles to fibrils to bundles), and mechanical response, including strain-stiffening and self-healing.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm where dispersity is intentionally used to program material hierarchy. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how polydispersity influences self-assembly pathways in block copolymer gels, directly contributing to the design of biomimetic soft materials. The resulting muscle-inspired fiber bundles could inform the development of actuators or scaffolds for tissue engineering.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied dispersity effects in blends or homopolymers, the deliberate engineering of block asymmetry and dispersity in a well-known thermogelling triblock system to create hierarchical, fiber-based gels is unexplored. This approach moves beyond mitigating dispersity as a flaw to actively harnessing it as a synthetic tool.

proposals.title: Programmable Nanostructures via Compensatory Orthogonal Interactions in Partially Miscible Block Polymer Blends
proposals.motivation: Achieving long-range ordered, complex nanostructures (e.g., gyroids) in block polymers typically requires strong segregation, limiting material choice and tunability. Partially miscible block pairs can create compositionally gradient interfaces with unique properties but are often too weakly segregated to form well-ordered structures. This work addresses the need for a general strategy to decouple interface design from thermodynamic stability, enabling new responsive and precisely defined nanomaterials.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating a strong, tunable secondary interaction (e.g., hydrogen-bonding arrays) orthogonally into one block of a partially miscible block polymer will provide a compensatory enthalpic driving force, stabilizing long-range ordered morphologies that are otherwise inaccessible and allowing independent control over interface gradient and nanostructure stability.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of model A-B diblock and A-B-A triblock polymers where A and B are intentionally partially miscible (e.g., poly(ethylene-alt-propylene)-b-poly(oligo(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate)). A complementary, strong interaction (e.g., a ureidopyrimidinone (UPy) hydrogen-bonding moiety) will be incorporated as a side-chain or end-group specifically in the B block. Phase behavior and morphology will be characterized via temperature-dependent SAXS, TEM, and rheology, correlating the strength of the orthogonal interaction (modulated by concentration and temperature) with the stability of gyroid and lamellar phases.
proposals.contribution: This will establish a new design paradigm for block polymers that separates the control of interfacial width from the thermodynamic stability of the nanostructure. It will provide a robust platform for creating responsive materials where morphology can be switched via the secondary interaction, with potential applications in templated nanomaterials and tunable membranes.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration of a powerful, switchable orthogonal interaction specifically to compensate for weak segregation in partially miscible blocks, creating a dual-control system for nanostructure programming, moves beyond traditional blending or simple copolymer design strategies.

proposals.title: Spinodal-Inspired Processing of Partially Miscible Block Polymers for Kinetically Trapped Gradient Nanostructures
proposals.motivation: Block polymer self-assembly is dominated by equilibrium morphologies from strongly immiscible blocks, limiting access to gradient or transient nanostructures with potentially unique properties. In metallurgy, controlled phase separation within miscibility gaps via spinodal decomposition and thermal tempering creates beneficial non-equilibrium microstructures. Adapting these principles to soft materials could unlock new structure-property relationships in block polymers, moving beyond the paradigm of strict immiscibility.
proposals.hypothesis: By designing block polymers with intentionally tuned, partial miscibility and applying controlled thermal or solvent histories inspired by alloy processing, we can access and kinetically trap gradient or modulated nanostructures via a spinodal-like decomposition pathway, rather than nucleation and growth.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of model A-B diblock and A-B-A triblock polymers (e.g., using poly(ethylene-alt-propylene)-b-poly(oligo(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate) [PEP-b-POEGMA] variants) where the Flory-Huggins interaction parameter (χ) is weakly temperature-dependent, creating a tunable miscibility gap. Using temperature-jump protocols within a controlled rheometer and SAXS chamber, we will map non-equilibrium pathways, mimicking alloy tempering. Solvent vapor annealing with mixed selectivity solvents will provide an alternative route to induce and arrest gradient compositions. Characterization will combine in-situ SAXS, TEM, and rheology to correlate processing history with nanostructure and mechanical properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new processing paradigm for block polymers based on controlled non-equilibrium pathways, directly imported from metallurgy. It will provide a foundational understanding of how to design and trap gradient nanostructures, leading to materials with novel mechanical (e.g., muscle fiber-like toughness) or transport properties. The findings will expand the design space for block polymers beyond equilibrium phase diagrams.
proposals.novelty: The approach fundamentally challenges the requirement for strong block immiscibility in polymer self-assembly, instead leveraging partial miscibility as a design feature. The explicit translation of metallurgical concepts like spinodal decomposition and tempering to block polymer processing is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Architecturally Encoded Dynamics: Reconfigurable Triply Periodic Networks from Stimuli-Responsive Comb Block Polymers
proposals.motivation: The self-assembly of block polymers (BCPs) into complex, non-equilibrium nanostructures like triply periodic minimal surfaces (TPMS) offers transformative potential for photonics and membranes. However, achieving dynamic, reversible transitions between such structures remains a significant challenge. Pre-2026 work on poloxamer hydrogels and ternary blends highlights the critical role of interfacial properties and composition gradients in controlling morphology and rheology, but these concepts have not been integrated with complex, non-linear polymer architectures to create reconfigurable materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthesizing comb-shaped block polymers with stimuli-responsive side chains will create compositionally graded interfaces that, under geometric constraints imposed by the comb architecture, will assemble into complex TPMS-like nanostructures capable of reversible, stimulus-triggered reconfiguration.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of comb block polymers via controlled radical polymerization, featuring a glassy polystyrene (PS) backbone block and a second block of poly[oligo(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate] (POEGMA) with tunable side chain length. The POEGMA block provides temperature-responsive miscibility with PS, akin to poloxamer behavior. We will systematically vary the grafting density and side chain length to control interfacial width and gradient. Phase behavior and dynamics will be characterized using temperature-controlled SAXS and rheology, building on our group's expertise with P407 hydrogels and ternary blend systems. Photocrosslinkable moieties will be incorporated into the POEGMA block to lock in transient structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a synthetic design principle for creating BCPs with architecturally defined, responsive interfaces. It will deliver the first library of comb BCPs capable of reversible, temperature-driven transitions between distinct complex nanostructures, providing new fundamental insights into the coupling of topology, interfacial gradients, and dynamics. The resulting materials will serve as a platform for next-generation smart membranes and photonic gels.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate fusion of non-linear polymer topology (comb architecture) with the established concept of a responsive, graded interface to encode dynamic assembly pathways, moving beyond static equilibrium structures in linear BCPs or simple binary blends.

proposals.title: Accessing Gradient and Transient Nanostructures in Block Polymers via Controlled Partial Miscibility and Thermal-Responsive Blending
proposals.motivation: Traditional block polymer self-assembly relies on strongly immiscible block pairs, limiting access to nanostructures with composition gradients or dynamic, temperature-responsive properties. Pre-2026 work in polymer blends and poloxamer hydrogels demonstrates that partial miscibility and thermal transitions can be leveraged to control morphology and rheology. Extending these principles to block polymer design could unlock new materials with adaptive mechanical behavior, akin to biological tissues like muscle fibers, for applications in soft robotics and injectable biomaterials.
proposals.hypothesis: By synthesizing block polymers where the constituent blocks are partially miscible or possess a lower critical solution temperature (LCST) type response, we can access non-equilibrium gradient nanostructures and thermally trigger reversible order-order transitions, moving beyond classical equilibrium morphologies.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of poly(ethylene oxide)-b-poly(oligo(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate) (PEO-b-POEGMA) diblock and triblock polymers, where the POEGMA side chain length is systematically varied to tune its miscibility with PEO. These will be characterized via temperature-dependent SAXS and rheology. We will further blend these with homopolymers (e.g., polystyrene, POEGMA) to create ternary systems, using the 'reverse poloxamer addition' concept to kinetically trap gradient interfaces. Photocrosslinkable versions will be used to freeze transient structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish design rules for creating block polymers with gradient and transient nanodomains, expanding the structural palette beyond sharp interfaces. It will demonstrate how thermal and compositional triggers can be used to dynamically modulate nanostructure and rheology, providing a new platform for stimuli-responsive gels and membranes.
proposals.novelty: While partial miscibility is typically avoided in block polymers, here we intentionally exploit it as a design parameter. Combining this with responsive blending techniques, adapted from recent work on poloxamers and ternary blends, to access non-classical nanostructures is a significant departure from conventional block copolymer design.

proposals.title: Field-Directed Kinetic Trapping of Non-Linear Block Polymer Architectures for Hierarchical Morphology Control
proposals.motivation: The equilibrium self-assembly of block polymers is well-established, but accessing non-equilibrium, hierarchical structures remains a significant challenge. Pre-2026 work on poloxamers and ternary blends demonstrates control over complex phases, yet methods to programmatically steer morphology during non-equilibrium processing are lacking. This limits the ability to create materials with tailored, multi-scale structures for advanced applications like tissue engineering scaffolds or responsive membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying an external field (electric or shear) during the rapid thermal quenching of non-linear block polymers (e.g., H-shaped or comb polymers) will selectively bias the kinetic pathway of microphase separation, enabling the programmable trapping of hierarchical, non-equilibrium morphologies not found at equilibrium.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of model non-linear block polymers (e.g., PS-PI H-shaped polymers) with controlled architectural variables. These polymers will be processed using a custom setup combining a Linkam shear stage or in-situ electro-rheological cell with rapid temperature quenching capabilities. The interplay between quench rate, field strength/direction, and polymer architecture will be systematically mapped using in-situ SAXS and ex-situ TEM to characterize the evolving and final morphologies.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first design principles linking non-linear polymer architecture, field parameters, and processing kinetics to non-equilibrium structural outcomes. It will provide a new methodology for creating on-demand, hierarchical polymer structures, expanding the functional palette for block polymer-based materials in nanotechnology and bioengineering.
proposals.novelty: The integration of complex macromolecular architecture as a tunable energy landscape with *in-situ* field guidance during quenching is a novel processing paradigm. This moves beyond equilibrium self-assembly or post-processing alignment, targeting the direct kinetic programming of morphology.

proposals.title: Sequential Field Programming of Non-Equilibrium Nanostructures in Photocrosslinkable Block Polymer Blends
proposals.motivation: Block polymer self-assembly is typically governed by equilibrium thermodynamics, limiting accessible morphologies to free-energy minima. Many complex, kinetically trapped nanostructures with potential applications in photonics and membranes are inaccessible. Inspired by protein folding chaperones, we propose that applying sequential, non-commuting energy inputs can guide assembly along designed kinetic pathways to capture these functional non-equilibrium states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a programmed sequence of orthogonal external fields—first a temperature quench to initiate microphase separation, followed by a specific shear field to align and distort the morphology, and finally UV light to crosslink and lock the structure—can reliably produce and stabilize targeted non-equilibrium nanostructures in block polymers.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of photocrosslinkable poloxamer (PEO-PPO-PEO) and polylactide-based triblock copolymers with tunable interaction parameters. These will be blended with homopolymers to form ternary systems, leveraging pre-2026 work on blend phase behavior. Assembly will be conducted in a custom rheometer with in-situ SAXS and UV source. A key algorithmic component is the design of the sequence: we will use pre-determined time-temperature-shear profiles, informed by coarse-grained simulations of chain dynamics under flow, to first create a metastable aligned state (e.g., a distorted gyroid or perforated lamella) before photo-crosslinking the PEO or methacrylate-containing domains to arrest the structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable methodology for 'pathway engineering' in soft matter, moving beyond equilibrium phase diagrams. It will demonstrate the deliberate creation and stabilization of at least one non-equilibrium morphology (e.g., a highly aligned or twisted network) with documented mechanical and transport properties. This contributes directly to the goal of programming material history for advanced function.
proposals.novelty: While individual fields (temperature, shear, light) are known to influence block polymers, the strategic, sequential application of multiple orthogonal fields as synthetic chaperones to navigate a kinetic pathway is a novel paradigm. This approach integrates the researcher's expertise in polymer blends, poloxamer rheology, and photocrosslinkable systems for a new purpose: kinetic control.

proposals.title: Pathway Engineering of Block Polymer Glasses: Mapping Quench Kinetics to Metastable Isotropic States
proposals.motivation: The properties of block polymers are typically dictated by their equilibrium microstructures, but kinetic pathways offer a largely untapped dimension for materials design. Pre-2026 work on poloxamer hydrogels and ternary blends demonstrates exquisite sensitivity of structure to processing history. This proposal seeks to systematically exploit kinetic arrest to create and characterize a new class of non-equilibrium polymeric glasses with tailored properties, moving beyond the paradigm of thermodynamic control.
proposals.hypothesis: That the specific thermal or solvent quenching pathway applied to a disordered block polymer melt can be programmed to trap distinct, reproducible metastable isotropic glassy states, whose structural signatures and physical properties are directly linked to the quench protocol and chain architecture.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of model AB diblock and BAB triblock polymers (e.g., PS-PI, PEO-PPO-PEO) with varying interaction parameters (χ) and molecular weights. Precise thermal quenches will be executed using rapid cooling stages, while solvent quenches will employ vapor sorption/desorption apparatus. The structural state will be characterized via temperature-dependent SAXS/WAXS to detect residual local order and dynamic heterogeneity. Rheology and calorimetry (DSC) will map the mechanical and thermal properties of the resulting glasses onto the quench parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for 'processing-structure-property' relationships in kinetically arrested block polymers. It will deliver a new materials design platform where functionality is imparted by pathway engineering, with potential applications in tunable membranes, optical coatings, and scaffolds for tissue engineering that mimic the hierarchical structure of muscle fibers.
proposals.novelty: While quenching is known for metals and glasses, its systematic application to trap and characterize *isotropic* states in self-assembling block polymers is novel. The approach merges the kinetic control concepts from hydrogel engineering with the deep thermodynamic understanding of block copolymer phase behavior to target metastable disordered states intentionally.

proposals.title: Kinetic Pathway Engineering in Poloxamer Triblock Copolymers for Rapid Formation of Metastable, Muscle Fiber-Inspired Nanostructures
proposals.motivation: Block polymer self-assembly is traditionally driven to equilibrium, limiting access to complex, non-equilibrium nanostructures with unique properties. Pre-2026 work on poloxamer hydrogels and ternary blends has revealed the profound influence of processing pathways on structure, but systematic control over kinetic pathways to trap metastable states remains a major challenge. This is critical for creating biomimetic, stimuli-responsive materials, such as those mimicking the hierarchical, anisotropic structure of muscle fibers.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing rapid, controlled solvent exchange and thermal quenching protocols on specifically designed poloxamer (PEO-PPO-PEO) triblock copolymers and their blends, we can bypass equilibrium morphologies and trap long-lived, anisotropic, and bicontinuous metastable nanostructures.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of poloxamers with varying PPO block lengths and PEO end-block fractions. Using a solvent vapor annealing (SVA) setup with precise humidity and temperature control, we will subject thin films to rapid solvent concentration jumps, mimicking 'reverse poloxamer addition' in solid state. The kinetic pathways will be monitored in situ via GISAXS and optical microscopy. We will further exploit photocrosslinkable moieties, inspired by prior work on bicontinuous microemulsions, to permanently arrest non-equilibrium structures formed during these rapid processes. Rheological characterization will link nanostructure to mechanical properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a processing-structure map for out-of-equilibrium self-assembly in a model commercial block polymer system. It will demonstrate a generalizable method to access and preserve complex nanostructures not found at equilibrium, directly contributing to the design of next-generation soft actuators, tissue engineering scaffolds, and tunable membranes. The findings will bridge the gap between kinetic theory and practical polymer processing.
proposals.novelty: While equilibrium phase behavior of poloxamers is well-studied, the deliberate use of rapid kinetic pathways to create and trap anisotropic, muscle fiber-like nanostructures in these simple triblock systems is unexplored. The combination of pathway-controlled SVA with in situ crosslinking to freeze non-equilibrium states represents a novel synthesis of Dr. Bates's recent methodological themes.

proposals.title: Covalent Integration of Functional Nanoparticles in Block Polymer Matrices via Orthogonal Click Chemistry in Self-Assembled Domains
proposals.motivation: Hybrid materials combining block polymer self-assembly with inorganic nanoparticles (NPs) or biomolecules often suffer from poor interfacial stability and component dispersion, limiting their functional performance in catalysis and nanomedicine. Current methods rely on physical blending or non-specific adsorption, which lead to component aggregation and weak interfaces under thermal or mechanical stress. A strategy to covalently and directionally tether functional components within specific polymer domains during self-assembly is critically needed to achieve robust, well-organized hybrid structures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthesizing block polymers with chemically orthogonal, click-reactive groups (e.g., azide, alkyne, tetrazine) selectively placed within one block will enable the covalent and spatially precise integration of complementary functionalized nanoparticles or proteins during microphase separation, creating hybrid materials with thermodynamically stable, covalently bonded interfaces.
proposals.method: We will synthesize poly(styrene)-b-poly(ethylene oxide) (PS-PEO) and poly(styrene)-b-poly(methyl methacrylate) (PS-PMMA) diblock copolymers where the PEO or PMMA blocks are functionalized with azide groups via controlled polymerization of tailored monomers. These polymers will be co-assembled with alkyne-functionalized gold or silica nanoparticles in selective solvents, or within ternary blends with homopolymers, to drive microphase separation into lamellar or bicontinuous structures. The azide-alkyne cycloaddition will be thermally initiated during assembly, covalently locking the NPs into the functionalized domains. Structure will be characterized by SAXS/TEM, and interface stability tested via temperature-dependent rheology.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general design principle for creating covalently integrated hybrid materials with precise component placement and robust interfaces. It will demonstrate how orthogonal chemistry, confined to specific polymer blocks, can direct hybrid assembly, moving beyond physical blending. The resulting materials are expected to show enhanced mechanical integrity and functional performance, providing a new platform for structured catalysts and therapeutic carriers.
proposals.novelty: While block polymer/nanoparticle composites are well-studied, the pre-programmed use of block-selective, orthogonal click chemistry to form covalent bonds *during* self-assembly, ensuring component integration is directed by thermodynamics *and* locked by kinetics, is a novel and non-obvious extension of Bates group expertise in blends, poloxamer gels, and polymeric microemulsions.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Coarse-Graining for Polydisperse Block Polymers: An N-body Simulation Analogy for Predicting Complex Phase Behavior
proposals.motivation: The self-assembly of polydisperse and multi-component block polymers, such as those with broad molecular weight distributions or complex architectures like triblocks and blends, governs the formation of complex nanostructures with tunable mechanical and transport properties. However, predicting their phase behavior is computationally prohibitive with standard self-consistent field theory (SCFT) or molecular simulations due to the vast parameter space. A new theoretical framework is urgently needed to map this complexity onto tractable models for designing advanced materials like thermoplastic elastomers, membranes, and photonic gels.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the hierarchical tree algorithms from astrophysical N-body simulations to polymer physics, we hypothesize that statistically similar polymer chains can be clustered into effective interaction groups, enabling the prediction of equilibrium and non-equilibrium assembly structures in polydisperse systems with accuracy comparable to detailed SCFT but at a fraction of the computational cost.
proposals.method: We will develop a coarse-grained simulation framework where polymer chains are represented as soft particles with interaction potentials derived from SCFT. A Barnes-Hut-type tree algorithm will be implemented to group distant or compositionally similar chains into effective super-particles, calculating their collective mean-field interactions. This hierarchical clustering will be based on parameters like chain length (N), composition (f), and architecture (e.g., A-B vs. A-B-A). The model will be validated against known phase diagrams for model systems (e.g., poloxamer PEO-PPO-PEO) and extended to complex ternary blends and photocrosslinkable systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational tool to efficiently screen the phase space of industrially relevant polydisperse and multi-component polymer systems, predicting novel morphologies beyond the classical phase diagram. It will establish a foundational link between astrophysical many-body techniques and soft matter physics, enabling the design of materials with targeted nanostructures for applications in drug delivery (hydrogels) and separation membranes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and algorithmic translation of hierarchical N-body methods from astrophysics to polymer assembly, a non-obvious cross-disciplinary analogy. This approach moves beyond standard coarse-graining by introducing adaptive, similarity-based clustering specifically for managing polydispersity and architectural diversity.

proposals.title: Decoupling Transport and Interfacial Properties in Solid Electrolytes via Polydisperse Triblock Polymer Architectures
proposals.motivation: Block polymers for energy devices, like solid-state batteries, face a fundamental trade-off: well-ordered, monodisperse materials offer clear transport pathways but often lack the mechanical robustness and interfacial adhesion required for durable devices. The prevailing design paradigm prioritizes narrow dispersity to achieve long-range order, potentially overlooking the functional benefits of controlled heterogeneity. This work challenges that paradigm by asking if deliberately engineered polydispersity can create hierarchical, multi-functional materials that integrate previously incompatible properties.
proposals.hypothesis: Controlled polydispersity in the mid-block of a poly(ethylene oxide)-based ABA triblock polymer (e.g., PEO-PPO-PEO analogues) will generate a hierarchical morphology where a distribution of chain lengths simultaneously enhances bulk ionic conductivity through a percolated, swollen network and optimizes electrode interfacial adhesion via a compositionally graded surface layer.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of poloxamer-like triblocks (A = polystyrene or a photocrosslinkable block, B = PEO) with controlled polydispersity in the PEO block via blending pre-synthesized fractions or using catalytic techniques that introduce a Schulz-Zimm distribution. These will be processed into membranes and characterized using SAXS and TEM to map morphology against dispersity. Rheology and impedance spectroscopy will measure mechanical properties and ionic conductivity. The photocrosslinkable system will allow us to lock in nascent bicontinuous microemulsion structures, inspired by prior work, to create stabilized, tortuous ion channels.
proposals.contribution: This will establish a new design principle for multi-scale functional polymers, moving beyond monodispersity as a strict requirement. It will provide a quantitative framework linking dispersity indices to decoupled material properties (transport vs. adhesion). The outcome is a direct strategy for creating single-material electrolytes that meet the complex demands of next-generation lithium-metal batteries.
proposals.novelty: While polydispersity is often seen as a defect, we propose its deliberate, strategic use as a primary design variable to create functional hierarchy. This inverts the standard approach in block polymer nanotechnology for electronics and energy, which almost exclusively seeks to minimize dispersity.

proposals.title: Thermoreversible, Sequence-Directed Assembly of Block Polymers via End-Functionalized Peptoid Duplexes
proposals.motivation: The use of DNA hybridization to program block polymer assembly offers exquisite specificity but suffers from hydrolytic instability, high cost, and incompatibility with organic solvents and high-temperature processing. A robust, scalable, and synthetically versatile alternative is needed to translate programmable self-assembly into practical materials. Peptoids, abiotic N-substituted glycine oligomers, offer a promising platform due to their sequence-specific folding, resistance to degradation, and compatibility with solid-phase synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sequence-defined peptoid oligomers, designed to form specific, thermoreversible duplexes via side-chain interactions, can be conjugated to block polymer chain ends to program their nanoscale organization with a fidelity and stability exceeding that of DNA-based systems.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of sequence-defined peptoid oligomers (8-12mers) with aromatic and hydrogen-bonding side chains designed for complementary duplex formation. These will be conjugated via click chemistry to the chain ends of model block polymers, including poloxamer (PEO-PPO-PEO) and polystyrene-poly(methyl methacrylate) (PS-PMMA) diblocks. Assembly will be characterized in thin films and bulk using temperature-controlled SAXS and TEM, correlating peptoid duplex melting transitions (via DSC and CD spectroscopy) with the formation and disassembly of ordered block polymer morphologies.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for programming block polymer nanostructures using synthetic, sequence-defined oligomers, decoupling assembly specificity from biological constraints. It will provide fundamental insights into how reversible, specific interactions at chain ends can direct bulk and thin-film morphology, enabling the design of responsive, reconfigurable materials with enhanced processibility.
proposals.novelty: While the Bates group has extensively studied ternary blends and complex phase behavior, using abiotic sequence-defined peptoids as programmable 'linkers' for block polymers is a novel, bio-inspired strategy that moves beyond traditional thermodynamic parameters like χ and N to encode assembly information directly into the polymer architecture.

proposals.title: Computational Design of Sequence-Defined Block Polymers via Protein Docking Algorithms for Programmed Self-Assembly
proposals.motivation: The precise self-assembly of block polymers into target nanostructures is limited by our ability to design specific, directional interactions between polymer blocks. Current methods rely on coarse chemical affinity, often leading to polymorphic or poorly defined structures. Adapting high-precision computational tools from structural biology could enable the rational design of polymer sequences that assemble with protein-like specificity.
proposals.hypothesis: Protein-protein docking algorithms, which predict binding interfaces and orientations based on complementary surface patches and interaction energies, can be adapted to design the interactive domains of sequence-defined block polymers, enabling the prediction and optimization of their self-assembly into pre-defined, complex nanostructures.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RosettaDock framework to model block polymer interactions. Polymer blocks will be represented as coarse-grained chains with defined sequences of interactive monomers (e.g., hydrogen-bonding, electrostatic, or hydrophobic units). The algorithm will sample relative orientations and conformations of two or more polymer blocks, scoring interactions using a modified energy function that incorporates Flory-Huggins χ parameters and specific directional interactions. This will be applied to design polymers targeting specific bicontinuous or cylindrical phases, with predictions validated by synthesizing candidate sequences (e.g., using polypeptide or sequence-defined synthetic polymers) and characterizing their assembly via SAXS and TEM.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new computational paradigm for the *de novo* design of self-assembling polymers, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will provide a direct link between monomer-level sequence information and resulting nanoscale morphology, significantly accelerating the discovery of polymers for advanced materials. The methodology could be extended to design interactive patches in polymer blends or for creating synthetic analogs of biological fibers and muscles.
proposals.novelty: While computational tools like SCFT are standard for block polymer phase behavior, applying protein docking algorithms to design sequence-specific interaction patches for assembly is a radical departure. This approach explicitly engineers binding interfaces at the monomer level, a concept not explored in pre-2026 polymer science.

proposals.title: Programming Single-Chain Nanoparticle Morphology via Sequence-Controlled Block Copolymer Folding in Selective Solvents
proposals.motivation: The self-assembly of block copolymers into well-defined nanostructures is a cornerstone of soft materials science, but it typically requires multiple chains organizing into micelles or mesophases. Achieving similar structural control at the single-chain level, akin to protein folding, would enable a new class of precision nanoscale objects with tailored functions. This bridges the gap between synthetic polymer physics and biomimetic folding, offering a route to monodisperse, single-chain nanoparticles (SCNPs) with predictable shapes for applications in catalysis, sensing, and as building blocks for hierarchical assemblies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing linear block copolymers with precisely sequenced blocks of varying hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity and incorporating intramolecular crosslinking units, we can program a single chain to fold into a specific, stable nanostructure (e.g., a core-shell sphere, a Janus particle, or a lamellar patch) in a selective solvent.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of linear ABC-type triblock copolymers using iterative RAFT polymerization for sequence control, incorporating a photocrosslinkable monomer (e.g., benzophenone methacrylate) in designated blocks. The folding process will be triggered by dissolving the polymer in a solvent selective for one block, inducing intramolecular collapse. Subsequent UV irradiation will lock the folded conformation via intramolecular crosslinking, as inspired by recent work on photocrosslinkable bicontinuous microemulsions. The morphology of the resulting SCNPs will be characterized using temperature-controlled liquid-phase AFM and cryo-TEM, while their solution assembly and rheology will be studied using dynamic light scattering and microrheology techniques analogous to those used for P407 hydrogels.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish design rules for the primary sequence-directed folding of synthetic polymers into non-random coil nanoparticles. It will contribute a new methodology for creating monodisperse, shape-persistent SCNPs and provide fundamental insights into the interplay between block sequence, solvent selectivity, and intramolecular crosslinking in determining single-chain morphology.
proposals.novelty: While SCNPs exist, programming their specific internal morphology (beyond a simple globule) via block sequence and controlled intramolecular phase separation is a novel, biomimetic approach. This moves beyond the statistical crosslinking of homopolymers or the multi-chain assembly of poloxamers to achieve protein-like structural specificity in a single synthetic chain.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Block Polymer Architectures from Target Phase Behavior Using Self-Consistent Field Theory and Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: The design of block polymers for specific applications, such as thermoplastic elastomers or membranes, relies on achieving a target nanoscale morphology dictated by the phase diagram. Current practice involves iterative synthesis and characterization to map phase space, which is slow and resource-intensive. An inverse design methodology, where polymer parameters are computed from a desired phase diagram, would dramatically accelerate materials discovery for advanced technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by coupling self-consistent field theory (SCFT) with a machine learning-based optimization framework, it is possible to solve the inverse problem: determining the necessary block polymer parameters (e.g., composition, Flory-Huggins χ parameter, block architecture) that will produce a user-specified target phase diagram.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline where a target phase diagram (e.g., specifying order-disorder transition temperatures and coexistence regions for lamellar, cylindrical, or bicontinuous phases) serves as the input. A neural network, trained on a large dataset of SCFT-generated forward-simulation results for diverse AB diblock and ABA triblock architectures, will act as a surrogate model to rapidly propose candidate polymer parameters. These candidates will be refined via an iterative SCFT-based optimization loop, minimizing the difference between the computed and target phase boundaries using gradient descent or genetic algorithms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational computational tool for the *a priori* design of block polymers with bespoke phase behavior. It will provide a direct link between application-driven morphological requirements and synthetic parameters, moving beyond the traditional trial-and-error approach. The resulting framework will be validated by synthesizing and characterizing a subset of predicted polymers, such as poloxamer-like PEO-PPO triblocks or photocrosslinkable systems, to confirm the predicted phase transitions.
proposals.novelty: While SCFT is well-established for predicting phase diagrams from known parameters, its application to the inverse design problem for arbitrary target diagrams is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in the integration of SCFT with machine learning surrogate models specifically to solve this inverse mapping, creating a non-intuitive, generative design tool for polymer chemists.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Triblock Terpolymers: Targeting Bicontinuous Microemulsions via Sequence-Controlled Polymerization
proposals.motivation: Traditional polymer synthesis often precedes assembly studies, limiting the systematic exploration of structure-property relationships. A grand challenge in soft materials is to invert this process: to first define a target nanostructure with specific mechanical or transport properties and then design the precise macromolecular architecture to achieve it. This is particularly relevant for complex, thermodynamically stable phases like bicontinuous microemulsions, which offer exceptional promise for templating, separation, and biomimetic applications but are difficult to access predictably.
proposals.hypothesis: By using self-consistent field theory (SCFT) calculations to map the phase space of ABC triblock terpolymers, we can identify precise monomer sequences and interaction parameters required to stabilize a bicontinuous microemulsion (BμE) phase, and subsequently realize these polymers using advanced polymerization techniques like photo-RAFT polymerization.
proposals.method: We will first employ SCFT to perform high-throughput screening of ABC triblock terpolymer parameter space (χ parameters, block volume fractions, sequence) to identify compositions predicted to form a BμE. Guided by these simulations, we will synthesize model polymers using iterative photo-RAFT polymerization, enabling precise control over block length and sequence. Key targets will include architectures where the mid-block is a photocrosslinkable moiety (e.g., derived from recent work on photocrosslinkable BμEs) to kinetically trap the structure. The assembly and phase behavior of synthesized polymers will be characterized via SAXS, TEM, and rheology to validate the inverse design approach.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a validated, simulation-guided framework for the inverse design of block polymers, moving beyond Edisonian discovery. It will yield a new library of sequence-defined triblocks that reliably form bicontinuous phases. Furthermore, it will demonstrate how targeted assembly can drive innovations in synthetic methodology, particularly in sequence control.
proposals.novelty: While SCFT has been used to explain observed phases, its direct use as a predictive tool to guide the synthesis of entirely new polymer architectures for a pre-specified complex phase represents a significant inversion of the standard research pipeline. The integration of this theoretical design with the precise synthesis of photocrosslinkable terpolymers to capture the BμE structure is a distinct advance.

proposals.title: Mapping the Phase Behavior of (Polystyrene-b-Polydiene)n Star-Block Copolymers: The Role of Architectural Asymmetry and Core Constraint
proposals.motivation: The phase behavior of linear A-B diblock copolymers is well-established, but the systematic study of star-block architectures, where multiple diblock arms radiate from a central core, remains incomplete. Understanding how arm number (n) and composition (f) influence self-assembly is crucial for designing nanostructured materials with tailored mechanical and transport properties, relevant to applications like thermoplastic elastomers and membranes. This work directly extends the Bates group's expertise in polymer blends and complex copolymer phase behavior to a more intricate, yet synthetically accessible, architectural family.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increasing the number of arms (n) in (A-B)n star-block copolymers at constant overall composition will systematically alter the phase diagram by amplifying the effect of the constrained core junction, stabilizing phases with lower interfacial curvature (e.g., lamellae) and potentially revealing novel network phases not found in linear analogs.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of (polystyrene-b-polyisoprene)n star-block copolymers (n = 2, 3, 4, 6, 8) via anionic polymerization using chlorosilane linking chemistry, varying the polystyrene volume fraction (fPS) systematically. Phase behavior will be mapped using temperature-dependent small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and dynamic mechanical spectroscopy. The experimental diagrams will be compared to self-consistent field theory (SCFT) calculations for star-block copolymers, specifically implementing polymer reference interaction site model (PRISM) theory to account for architectural constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive experimental phase diagrams for a homologous series of star-block copolymers, establishing clear structure-property relationships between arm number and ordered morphology. It will provide critical test data for theoretical models of architecturally complex polymers and identify new avenues for creating nanostructured materials with controlled connectivity and properties, such as tunable soft gels or reinforced elastomers.
proposals.novelty: While star polymer thermodynamics have been studied, a systematic experimental mapping of (A-B)n star-block phase space as a function of both n and f is unprecedented. The integration of precise synthesis, rheological characterization (linking to 'muscle, fibers' themes), and advanced SCFT modeling for these specific architectures represents a significant step beyond the study of linear diblocks or miktoarm stars.

proposals.title: Mapping the Order-Disorder Transition in Photocrosslinkable Bicontinuous Microemulsions and Poloxamer Gels via Dynamical Mechanical Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: The order-disorder transition (ODT) is a critical phenomenon in block polymer self-assembly, dictating material structure and properties. While small-angle scattering is the standard for ODT detection, it provides limited direct insight into the viscoelastic changes that govern processability and function, especially in complex, multi-component systems like bicontinuous microemulsions and thermoresponsive gels. A rheological methodology to precisely locate and characterize the ODT would bridge the gap between equilibrium morphology and mechanical performance, which is essential for designing advanced soft materials for biomedical and engineering applications.
proposals.hypothesis: The ODT in architecturally complex soft matter systems, specifically photocrosslinkable polymeric bicontinuous microemulsions and reverse-poloxamer hydrogels, will manifest as a distinct, rheologically-defined transition characterized by a precipitous drop in the elastic modulus (G') and a shift in terminal relaxation behavior, which can be systematically mapped as a function of temperature and composition.
proposals.method: We will employ temperature-sweep dynamical mechanical spectroscopy (oscillatory rheology) on a strain-controlled rheometer with a parallel-plate geometry. For the photocrosslinkable bicontinuous microemulsions (based on prior work), frequency sweeps at discrete temperatures will bracket the suspected ODT, with the transition identified by the temperature where G' ~ ω^0.5 (disordered melt) replaces the low-frequency plateau (ordered structure). For P407 (Poloxamer 407) systems, including reverse-addition formulations, temperature ramps at fixed frequency will pinpoint the gel-sol transition via a sharp crossover of G' and G''. Time-temperature superposition will be applied near the ODT to construct master curves and extract relaxation times.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust rheological protocol for identifying the ODT in non-canonical block polymer architectures, moving beyond neat diblock melts. It will directly correlate the loss of long-range order with macroscopic viscoelasticity in photocrosslinkable bicontinuous structures and complex poloxamer blends, providing critical design rules for materials where thermal processing or in situ gelation is required.
proposals.novelty: While rheology is used for ODT in simple diblocks, its systematic application to dynamically evolving, photocrosslinkable bicontinuous microemulsions and to the gelation transition of reverse-addition poloxamers is unexplored. This approach uniquely probes the mechanical consequences of disorder in these complex, multi-phase systems.

ideas.title: Link fundamental phase behavior to new materials applications
ideas.description: Discover and develop novel material applications from fundamental studies of polymer-polymer phase behavior.
ideas.sourceTitle: Polymer-Polymer Phase Behavior

ideas.title: Theory-guided design of ternary polymer blends to target and control fluctuation spectra
ideas.description: This research employs a combined theoretical and experimental approach to rationally design ternary polymer blends for modifying fluctuation spectra. First, SCFT and PRISM theory are used to model the impact of a third component (homopolymer or copolymer) on the structure factor and phase diagram of a model block copolymer system. The computational screening identifies promising blend compositions that amplify or suppress fluctuations in a targeted manner. These predictions are then validated experimentally via a tightly coupled synthesis and characterization loop, using small-angle X-ray scattering to measure the fluctuation spectra directly, thereby creating a robust design platform.

ideas.title: Use self-assembly principles from colloidal science
ideas.description: Apply concepts and forces governing colloidal crystal formation to guide block copolymer assembly.
ideas.sourceTitle: Block Copolymers—Designer Soft Materials

ideas.title: Quenching-Induced Self-Assembly of Polymers with Engineered Polydispersity
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the formation of non-equilibrium isotropic materials through rapid temperature quenching of polymer melts or solutions with intentionally engineered, non-uniform molecular weight distributions. By systematically varying both the quenching protocol and the polydispersity profile, we aim to discover how a controlled spread of chain lengths influences the kinetic pathways and final arrested structures, such as glasses or gels, that result from the non-equilibrium processing. This approach challenges the paradigm that monodispersity is essential for controlled assembly by treating polydispersity as a key variable to tune the material's response to extreme processing conditions. The goal is to establish design rules for creating novel self-assembled materials whose functional properties are directly encoded by both the polydispersity and the quenching history.

ideas.title: Programmable Fluctuation Landscapes in Sequence-Defined Block Copolymers
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes block copolymers where one or more blocks are functionalized with programmable nucleic acid sequences, creating a library of materials with precise, sequence-encoded interaction strengths. By systematically varying both the polymer composition (volume fraction, chemistry) and the nucleic acid sequence design, we can map how sequence-specific binding energy modulates fluctuation spectra and phase behavior across different morphologies (e.g., lamellae, cylinders, gyroids). This allows us to test if fluctuation-mediated phenomena, like order-disorder transitions or defect dynamics, can be universally controlled by sequence design, irrespective of the underlying polymer chemistry. The ultimate goal is to establish design rules for 'fluctuation engineering' to create materials with tailored dynamic responsiveness and stability.

ideas.sourceTitle: Polymersomes: tough vesicles made from diblock copolymers.

ideas.title: Adapt phase field modeling from metallurgy
ideas.description: Simulate the complex kinetic pathways of multiblock polymer self-assembly and processing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Multiblock Polymers: Panacea or Pandora’s Box?

ideas.title: Programmable Quenching of Block Polymers to Design and Characterize Metastable Isotropic Glasses
ideas.description: This research direction combines the kinetic pathway control of block polymer self-assembly with the quenching characterization of isotropic materials to establish a platform for designing 'metastable isotropic glasses'. By applying precisely engineered thermal or solvent quenches to block polymers with specific chain architectures and interactions, we can trap them in a disordered, isotropic state that is far from equilibrium. We will then systematically characterize the structural signatures (e.g., residual local order, dynamic heterogeneity) and physical properties (e.g., mechanical, optical) of these glasses as a function of the quenching pathway. The synergy enables the creation of a new class of polymeric materials whose properties are dictated not by thermodynamic minima, but by the intentionally selected kinetic arrest conditions, linking processing history directly to final state functionality.

ideas.title: Achieve rapid, out-of-equilibrium self-assembly processes
ideas.description: Control and exploit kinetic pathways for block polymer self-assembly to create non-equilibrium, metastable nanostructures.
ideas.sourceTitle: 50th Anniversary Perspective: Block Polymers—Pure Potential

ideas.title: Critical molecular weight is universal for networks

ideas.title: Relax the requirement for immiscible block pairs

ideas.title: Systematic study of star-block copolymer phase behavior
ideas.description: Experimentally map phase diagrams for (A-B)n star architectures as a function of arm number and composition.

ideas.title: Scale down to single-chain nanoparticle self-assembly
ideas.description: Design single block polymer chains that fold into defined nanostructures, mimicking protein folding.
ideas.sourceTitle: 50th Anniversary Perspective: Block Polymers—Pure Potential

ideas.title: Machine Learning-Guided Discovery of Fluctuation Phenomena in Complex Multiblock Copolymers
ideas.description: This research direction integrates high-throughput coarse-grained simulations of triblock and multiblock copolymers with machine learning to establish a predictive framework for fluctuation phenomena. The ML models are trained on simulation data to learn the complex, non-linear relationships between architectural variables (e.g., block sequence, compositional asymmetry, number of blocks) and metrics of fluctuation effects, such as order-disorder transition shifts and interfacial broadening. This enables inverse design, where desired fluctuation-stabilized or fluctuation-suppressed morphologies are specified, and the model proposes candidate architectures. The synergy lies in using ML to decode the 'fluctuation fingerprint' of complex architectures, accelerating the discovery of polymers with tailored thermal and mechanical responses governed by these soft-matter physics.

ideas.title: Polydisperse Block Polymers as Multi-Scale Functional Materials for Energy and Electronics
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the paradigm that narrow dispersity is essential for functional block polymers in energy and electronic devices. Instead, it deliberately engineers controlled polydispersity in one or more blocks to create hierarchical structures, where a distribution of chain lengths drives the formation of multi-scale morphologies. This approach can decouple material properties, using, for example, a dispersity gradient to enhance ionic conductivity in a bulk phase while a specific block length fraction optimizes interfacial adhesion or electronic contact at an electrode. The outcome is a new design strategy for next-generation batteries, photovoltaics, and sensors where complex property sets are integrated into a single, processable material.

ideas.title: Study non-equilibrium dynamics in complex polymer architectures
ideas.description: Extend non-equilibrium dynamics analysis beyond model systems to more complex polymer architectures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Polymer-Polymer Phase Behavior

ideas.title: Compensatory Orthogonal Interactions in Partially Miscible Block Polymers for Programmable Nanostructures
ideas.description: This research designs block polymers with intentionally selected, partially miscible block pairs to create compositionally gradient interfaces. To overcome the inherent weakness of the segregation driving force, a complementary, strong, and tunable secondary interaction (such as a hydrogen-bonding array or metal-ligand complex) is incorporated specifically into one block. This orthogonal interaction provides a powerful and switchable enthalpic contribution to the free energy, enabling the stabilization of long-range ordered morphologies (e.g., gyroids, lamellae) that would otherwise be inaccessible. The system allows independent control over the gradient character via the block pair chemistry and the nanostructure stability/order via the secondary interaction, creating a robust platform for responsive and precisely defined nanomaterials.

ideas.title: Non-linear Block Polymers with Responsive Interfacial Gradients for Dynamic, Reconfigurable Nanostructures
ideas.description: This research direction proposes the synthesis of block polymers featuring intricate, non-linear architectures (e.g., comb, cyclic, dendritic, or H-shaped) where the constituent blocks are deliberately designed to be partially miscible or environmentally responsive. The complex topology imposes unique geometric constraints on the phase separation process, forcing the formation of interfaces with controlled composition and property gradients that vary in three dimensions. By applying external stimuli such as temperature, light, or solvent vapor, these gradient interfaces can be dynamically modulated, leading to reversible and predictable transformations between complex, non-equilibrium nanostructures like triply periodic networks or hierarchically ordered defect arrays. This synergy aims to establish a new paradigm of 'architecturally encoded dynamic materials' where both molecular shape and interfacial responsiveness are co-engineered to achieve unprecedented spatiotemporal control over nanoscale assembly.

ideas.title: Field-Guided Quenching of Non-Linear Block Polymers to Program Non-Equilibrium Hierarchies
ideas.description: This research direction combines the synthesis of block polymers with intricate, non-linear architectures (e.g., H-shaped, comb, or cyclic blocks) with the application of external fields (electric, flow, or magnetic) during rapid thermal quenching. The complex polymer provides a rich, multi-scale free energy landscape with numerous metastable states, while the field applied during the non-equilibrium processing acts as a selective bias to steer the evolving morphology along a specific kinetic pathway. This allows for the programmable creation of hierarchical, non-equilibrium structures that are not accessible by equilibrium self-assembly or quenching alone, such as metastable perforated layers or oriented micellar networks from the same precursor material. The core challenge is to map the interplay between architectural variables, field type/strength, and quench rate to establish design principles for on-demand structural fabrication.

ideas.title: Adapting Phase-Separation Control from Metallurgy for Gradient Block Polymer Nanostructures
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that block polymers require strongly immiscible blocks. By drawing an analogy from metallurgy—where controlled phase separation in partially miscible alloys (via spinodal decomposition) is used to create gradient or modulated microstructures—we adapt this solution to polymer science. The analogous solution involves precise management of thermal history and composition within a miscibility gap to stabilize beneficial non-equilibrium structures. The adapted research idea is to design block polymers with intentionally partially miscible or responsive blocks and employ controlled processing protocols (thermal, solvent, or field-based) inspired by alloy tempering to access and kinetically trap gradient, transient, or metastable nanostructures. This approach aims to unlock new structure-property relationships in soft materials, moving beyond the equilibrium morphologies dictated by strict immiscibility.

ideas.title: In situ TEM Tomography of Shear-Induced Ordering Kinetics in Block Copolymers
ideas.description: This research direction combines in situ rheology with transmission electron microscopy tomography to study the kinetics of shear-induced long-range ordering in block copolymers. By applying controlled shear directly within the TEM and acquiring tomographic datasets at sequential time points, the experiment captures the real-space, three-dimensional evolution of the microstructure. This approach uniquely distinguishes between the growth of the thermodynamically favored aligned structure and the dissolution dynamics of transient, fluctuating domains that coexist during the ordering pathway. The resulting 4D (3D + time) data provides an unprecedented mechanistic understanding of ordering kinetics, directly correlating local shear conditions with the nucleation, growth, and annihilation events that lead to macroscopic alignment.

ideas.title: Interface-Specific Block Polymers for Covalently Integrated Hybrid Materials
ideas.description: This approach designs and synthesizes block polymers with pre-programmed, chemically orthogonal binding sites within specific block domains to direct the covalent and directional integration of functional components during self-assembly. By moving beyond simple physical blending, this method ensures robust, thermodynamically stable interfaces between the polymer matrix and inorganic nanoparticles or biomolecules. The resulting hybrid materials exhibit superior structural integrity, precise spatial organization of components, and enhanced functional performance for applications in catalysis, sensing, and nanomedicine.

ideas.title: Challenge the necessity of narrow polymer dispersity
ideas.description: Explore if controlled polydispersity can be a design feature, not a flaw, for creating novel self-assembled materials.
ideas.sourceTitle: 50th Anniversary Perspective: Block Polymers—Pure Potential

ideas.title: Programming Kinetic Pathways in Block Polymers Using Sequential Energy Inputs
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solutions from synthetic biology and protein folding, where chaperones and sequential energy inputs guide polypeptides to specific, often metastable, functional conformations. The analogous solution involves using programmed, time-dependent external fields (light, electric, shear) or chemical triggers as 'synthetic chaperones' for block copolymers. By applying these inputs in a specific sequence, the assembly kinetics can be steered along a designed pathway, bypassing the thermodynamic minimum to rapidly capture and stabilize a desired non-equilibrium nanostructure, such as a perforated lamella or a twisted gyroid, which would be inaccessible under equilibrium conditions.

ideas.title: Test stability of cross-linked micelles under varied conditions
ideas.description: Investigate the chemical and thermal stability of the cross-linked PB cores in different environments.

ideas.title: Programmable Dispersity: Synthetically Encoding Distribution Profiles to Direct Self-Assembly Pathways
ideas.description: This research program posits that a polymer's chain length distribution is a high-dimensional parameter that can be synthetically programmed to encode material function. Instead of treating dispersity as an uncontrolled flaw, we will use advanced iterative synthesis to create precise libraries of polymers with designed dispersity profiles—such as bimodal, narrow Gaussian, or intentionally skewed distributions. These well-defined 'dispersity polymers' will serve as inputs to systematically investigate and establish causal relationships between the shape of the molecular weight distribution and the resulting self-assembled nanostructures, their dynamics, and their bulk mechanical or functional properties. The goal is to develop a design framework where a target material behavior dictates a required dispersity profile, which is then synthesized directly.

ideas.description: Apply theories of rubber elasticity to model the cross-linked wormlike core.

ideas.description: Use phase-field methods, common in alloy solidification, to model block copolymer microstructure evolution.

ideas.title: Field-Guided Quenching of DNA-Origami-Programmed Block Polymers for Metastable Nanostructures
ideas.description: This research direction combines the principles of DNA origami and sequence-specific interactions with the dynamic control of the quenching inverse problem. We propose to synthesize block polymers where specific blocks are functionalized with single-stranded DNA 'handles', enabling programmable, addressable interactions during self-assembly. By applying a tunable external field (e.g., electric, magnetic, or shear) during a rapid temperature quench of these polymers from a disordered melt, we can bias the kinetic pathways of association. This allows us to not only program the target structure via sequence design but also to actively guide and freeze-in complex, metastable nanostructures that possess the specificity of DNA-directed assembly but the non-equilibrium structural features achievable only through field-controlled quenching.

ideas.title: Apply renormalization group from critical phenomena
ideas.description: Employ renormalization group techniques from critical phenomena to study polymer blend phase transitions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Polymer-Polymer Phase Behavior

ideas.title: Adapting Astrophysical N-body Simulation Methods for Multi-component Polymer Assembly
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to develop a theoretical framework for predicting the self-assembly of block polymers with many components or broad molecular weight distributions by drawing an analogy from computational astrophysics. In astrophysics, N-body simulations solve the many-body problem for gravitating stars by using hierarchical tree algorithms to group distant interactions, making large-scale simulations tractable. The adapted solution applies this coarse-graining, hierarchical logic to polymer physics. A new theoretical model will be developed where polymers are not treated individually but are statistically clustered into effective interaction groups based on similarity in chain length, composition, or architecture. This framework aims to map the vast parameter space of polydisperse, multi-component systems onto a manageable set of order parameters and interaction potentials, enabling the prediction of complex phase diagrams and non-equilibrium assembly structures that are currently computationally prohibitive to model with atomistic or standard self-consistent field theory approaches.

ideas.title: Applying Dynamical Systems Control Theory to Shear-Induced Disorder
ideas.description: This idea inverts the role of shear from ordering to disordering by drawing an analogy from nonlinear dynamics and control theory. In that field, systems are deliberately driven from order into chaos or between attractors using precise perturbations. The analogous solution involves using high-amplitude, timed forcing to break the feedback loops of a stable state. Adapted to the materials context, the research would apply high shear rates or specific shear profiles as controlled dynamical perturbations to a system with inherent equilibrium order (e.g., a colloidal crystal, liquid crystal, or self-assembled gel). The objective is to see if these targeted shear 'inputs' can act as a control parameter to destroy the original order and induce and characterize distinct, sustained non-equilibrium states, effectively mapping the shear-forcing phase diagram to material response.

ideas.title: Model dynamics of phase transitions between complex structures
ideas.description: Study the kinetic pathways and barriers for transitions like gyroid to lamellae with increasing segregation.

ideas.description: Start with a target phase diagram and work backwards to compute the required block polymer parameters.

ideas.title: Apply Protein Docking Algorithms to Program Block Polymer Assembly
ideas.description: This idea transfers a solution from computational structural biology to block polymer design. The analogous problem is predicting how two proteins dock together to form a specific complex, which is solved using algorithms that sample conformations and score interaction energies to find the most stable binding mode. This solution is adapted by using protein docking algorithms (e.g., RosettaDock) as a design tool for block polymers. The algorithms would be used to computationally model and optimize the sequence-defined interactive 'patches' or domains on synthetic polymer blocks, predicting their binding interfaces, orientation, and stability. This allows for the in-silico design of polymer sequences that will self-assemble into target nanostructures with precise spatial organization, guided by engineered complementary interactions modeled on protein docking principles.

ideas.description: Construct a complete morphological phase diagram as a function of polymer concentration and PEO weight fraction.

ideas.title: In-situ rheo-SAXS validation of superposition model for equilibrium ordered morphology under shear and temperature
ideas.description: This research direction employs in-situ rheo-SAXS with precise temperature control to experimentally test the superposition model for equilibrium ordered morphologies. By applying controlled shear fields while simultaneously monitoring the SAXS structure factor, the experiment can distinguish the static lamellar scattering signature from the diffuse, fluctuating component associated with spinodal patterns. This allows for the direct validation of the superposition hypothesis by quantifying how each component's intensity, orientation, and correlation length evolves with shear rate and temperature, linking specific structural motifs to the macroscopic viscoelastic response. The approach moves beyond static observation or pure rheology to dynamically probe the interplay between equilibrium and non-equilibrium processes in complex morphologies.

ideas.title: Study polymersome stability in biological media
ideas.description: Test the mechanical and chemical stability of polymersomes in serum or blood.

ideas.title: Sequence-Defined Peptoid Oligomers as Robust, Scalable Programmers for Block Polymer Nanostructure
ideas.description: This redesigned approach uses synthetic sequence-defined peptoid oligomers, rather than natural nucleic acids, to program the spatial organization of block polymers. Peptoids are abiotic polymers with side-chain sequences that can be designed to form specific, reversible duplexes via side-chain stacking and hydrogen bonding, analogous to DNA hybridization but with enhanced stability and processibility. These oligomers are conjugated to block polymer chain ends or junctions, where their programmable association drives precise, thermoreversible nanoscale assembly. The system overcomes the key limitations of DNA by utilizing scalable, low-cost solid-phase synthesis of chemically diverse, non-hydrolyzable peptoids that are compatible with a wide range of polymer processing conditions and organic solvents.

ideas.title: In-situ Rheo-SAXS-Guided Kinetic Pathway Engineering for Block Polymers
ideas.description: This research direction integrates in-situ rheo-SAXS with temperature control to actively steer rapid, out-of-equilibrium self-assembly processes in block polymers. By applying precisely defined shear and thermal histories while simultaneously monitoring the evolving nanostructure via SAXS, the kinetic pathways can be manipulated in real-time. This allows researchers to deliberately navigate the energy landscape, using shear to bypass equilibrium phases and trap long-lived, metastable structures with novel properties. The core advance is the transition from passive observation of kinetics to active, feedback-informed engineering of non-equilibrium nanostructures based on direct structural readouts.

ideas.title: Employ microfluidic platforms for rapid self-assembly screening
ideas.description: Use microfluidic devices to create gradients of conditions and rapidly map block polymer phase behavior.

ideas.title: Decoupling shear alignment from true ordering: Pathways and yield dynamics below the ODT
ideas.description: This study probes the kinetics of shear-induced ordering by explicitly distinguishing between flow alignment and genuine long-range order development. We employ a coupled experimental system of in-situ rheo-small-angle X-ray scattering (rheo-SAXS) with large amplitude oscillatory shear (LAOS) to capture non-linear viscoelastic signatures concurrent with structural evolution. A key protocol involves rapid, step-wise cessation of shear at various stages to test the stability and longevity of the induced structure. By correlating the post-cessation structural relaxation, non-linear rheological parameters, and the scattering patterns, we map the dynamic pathway from disordered and aligned states to a truly ordered phase, identifying the critical yield stress and deformation conditions required for the transition.

ideas.title: Programming Hierarchical Assembly via Controlled Polydispersity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the foundational assumption that narrow polymer dispersity is always necessary for precise self-assembly. Drawing an analogy from Ecosystem Ecology, where biodiversity is a designed feature for ecosystem function, not a flaw, we adapt the solution of 'functional trait diversity' to polymer science. The adapted research idea is to program hierarchical assembly via controlled polydispersity. Instead of seeking monodisperse samples, we would intentionally synthesize or blend polymers with precisely defined distributions in molecular weight, block length, and chemical functionality. This 'polymeric biodiversity' would be designed so that different sub-populations within the mixture play specific, complementary roles during self-assembly—akin to different species in an ecosystem—enabling the bottom-up construction of materials with novel hierarchical structures, adaptive properties, or enhanced toughness that are inaccessible to monodisperse systems.

ideas.title: Non-Thermal Assembly via Programmable Solvent Flows
ideas.description: This idea challenges the supremacy of thermal annealing by developing assembly methods that replace thermal energy with the directed kinetic energy of solvent flows. The analogy is drawn from geomorphology and river engineering, where flowing water sorts and arranges sediments into precise patterns without any thermal input. The adapted solution is to design programmable solvent flow regimes in microfluidic or larger-scale assembly chambers. These controlled flows—varying in velocity, shear, and pattern—will provide the directional forces to sort, transport, align, and fuse nanoparticles, polymers, or colloidal building blocks into the desired superstructure, using solvent interactions as the primary binding and guiding mechanism instead of thermal diffusion.

ideas.title: Data-Grounded Theory for Synthetically Feasible Complex Polymer Assemblies
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop, computationally assisted theoretical framework to predict the self-assembly of complex block polymers. The approach begins by defining a constrained design space of multi-component or polydisperse systems that are synthetically tractable using modern polymerization techniques. High-throughput coarse-grained simulations, guided by active learning, generate a focused dataset of assembly outcomes for these specific architectures. This data is then used to train interpretable machine learning models that serve as rapid, accurate predictors of phase behavior, directly linking theoretical exploration to actionable experimental guidelines.

ideas.title: Quantitative 4D-STEM Tomography for Direct Validation of Static-Fluctuating Superposition in Block Copolymers
ideas.description: This fused research direction combines the 3D real-space imaging capability of TEM tomography with the explicit goal of validating a superposition model for equilibrium ordered morphologies. The synergy lies in using 4D-STEM during the tilt-series acquisition to collect nanodiffraction patterns at each voxel, enabling the simultaneous reconstruction of the static lamellar structure and a 3D map of local scattering variance indicative of spinodal fluctuations. This approach directly tests the superposition hypothesis by providing a spatially resolved, quantitative correlation between the static template and the amplitude and spatial distribution of embedded fluctuations within the same sample volume. It moves beyond speculation to furnish direct, causal evidence for how competing interactions manifest as superimposed order in soft matter systems.

ideas.title: Dynamic Block Polymer Editing via Reversible Covalent Chemistry
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a block polymer's sequence is fixed after synthesis by drawing an analogy from synthetic biology's DNA recombination systems (e.g., Cre-loxP). In those systems, enzymes dynamically cut and rejoin DNA at specific sites to rewrite sequences. Adapted to polymer science, the solution is to create block polymers where specific junction points contain stimuli-responsive, reversible covalent bonds. Post-polymerization, applying a tailored stimulus (a specific catalyst, light of a particular wavelength, or a change in pH) would act like a 'recombinase,' selectively cleaving and reforming bonds at these designated sites. This allows the polymer's block sequence, topology, or composition to be reconfigured, enabling dynamic, post-synthetic editing of the polymer chain to switch material properties such as toughness, permeability, or self-assembly behavior.
